# Implementation Notes

## Changed

- Added a 256-entry direct-mapped instruction replay table inside `rtl/if_stage.sv`, with valid bits, full 32-bit PC tags, and 32-bit instruction words.
- Added a registered lookahead replay candidate for the expected next fetch PC. Current-cycle IF selection only chooses between live `imem_data` and the previously registered candidate; it does not read or tag-compare the replay table combinationally.
- Live `imem_ready` fetches always refresh the replay entry for the current PC. A same-PC fill bypass updates the candidate on held-PC cycles so a just-fetched held instruction can be replayed if the external imem stalls next cycle.

title: Compressed Resetless I-Fetch Replay Tags
motivation:
The current best fitness comes from the registered lookahead instruction
replay predictor: it preserves most of the 256-entry replay table's large
random-iStall cycle reduction while moving the same-cycle tag lookup off the
IF decision path. That win also makes the replay table one of the remaining
high-leverage timing structures. The table still stores a full 32-bit PC tag
for every entry even though the direct-mapped index already encodes
`pc[9:2]` and all reachable fetch PCs are word-aligned. It also resets the
tag and instruction arrays even though the valid bits alone determine
whether those payloads can be consumed.

Recent micro-opt results show that exact timing-only cleanups can matter:
the 16-bit segmented RVFI order counter improved Fmax without changing
CoreMark cycles, while broader datapath rewrites and smaller replay tables
lost either hit rate or placement quality. Compressing and de-resetting the
existing replay metadata is the same kind of narrow micro-optimization: keep
the current replay behavior and cycle count, but reduce IF table width,
reset fanout, and registered-candidate comparison logic.
hypothesis:
Keep the 256-entry registered lookahead replay predictor and its live-imem
first policy unchanged, but store only the non-index PC tag bits in each
entry. With `REPLAY_INDEX_BITS=8`, replace the full 32-bit `replay_tag`
array with a `replay_tag_hi` array holding `pc[31:10]`; `pc[9:2]` is already
the array index, and `pc[1:0]` is always zero for architecturally reachable
fetches because misaligned branch/JAL/JALR targets trap and suppress the
redirect. The lookahead fill, lookup, and fill-bypass paths should compare
only these high tag bits while retaining the existing guard that the
registered candidate corresponds to the current fetch PC.

Also remove the reset writes to replay tag and instruction payload arrays.
Reset clears only `replay_valid` plus the registered candidate state; invalid
entries may hold arbitrary tag/data bits because they are never selected.
Live `io_imemReady=1` cycles still use `io_imemData` and refresh the replay
entry. `io_imemReady=0` cycles still replay only on a valid matching
registered candidate, otherwise preserving the current PC-hold and IF-bubble
behavior. Branch/JAL prediction, redirect priority, hazard-unit fetch-ready
semantics, RVFI instruction reporting, top-level IO, pending-store behavior,
divider interlock, and channel-0-only retirement remain unchanged.

The expected gain is a modest Fmax improvement from shortening the replay
tag read/compare path, reducing table storage width, and removing reset
pressure from the replay payload memories, with no intended change to
CoreMark iterations/cycle.

changes.description:
Compress the replay table tag from a full 32-bit PC to the high
non-index bits `pc[31:10]`, and update the registered lookahead lookup,
fill, and fill-bypass comparisons accordingly. Clear only replay valid
bits and registered candidate state on reset; leave replay tag and
instruction payload arrays resetless because invalid entries cannot be
selected. Preserve the 256-entry table size, live-imem priority,
registered replay-hit behavior, static backward-branch/direct-JAL
prediction, redirect priority, and `fetch_ready` contract.

Clarify only the IF/ID prediction comments to note that replay entries
are tagged by the high PC bits plus the direct-mapped index, and that
replay tag/instruction payload arrays are resetless behind valid bits.
Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality
defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: MEM-Stage Load Replay Behind I-Fetch Replay
motivation:
The current best core gets its large gain from the registered lookahead
instruction replay table: random iStall bubbles are often converted into
useful fetches without putting a same-cycle table lookup on the IF critical
path. That leaves the data side as the cleanest remaining structural stall
source. A load still freezes the whole in-order backend whenever
`io_dmemReady` is low, even when the same normal-RAM word was recently read
in CoreMark's compact 6 KB working set.

The earlier standalone MEM-stage load replay experiment was the best
structural signal after the store slot: it reduced CoreMark cycles with a
small, conservative table located away from the IF predictor path and did
not require load data to feed the EX operand mux. It was not combined with
the current registered instruction replay best. Combining the two targets
independent random-stall sources: IF replay hides instruction stalls, while
MEM replay can retire repeated loads during data-memory stalls.
hypothesis:
Add a small normal-RAM load replay buffer in the MEM stage, integrated with
the current pending-store slot and registered IF replay design. Use a
direct-mapped table, for example 16 entries indexed by aligned word address,
with valid bits, word tags, and raw 32-bit memory words returned by previous
accepted loads. On `io_dmemReady=1`, loads keep using live `io_dmemRData`
exactly as today and refresh the replay entry for their aligned word. On
`io_dmemReady=0`, a legal non-trapping normal-RAM load may complete from the
replay table on a valid tag hit: suppress the live dmem read, use the cached
raw word for sign/zero extension and RVFI `mem_rdata`, and do not assert
`mem_wait`.

Replay must remain conservative. It is disabled while the pending store slot
is valid, for MMIO/marker/UART addresses, for misaligned or trapping memory
operations, and for stores. Normal-RAM stores update or invalidate any
matching replay entry before they retire or enqueue into the pending store
slot, so later replayed loads cannot observe stale data. Replay misses and
all nonbufferable accesses preserve the current dmem wait behavior. This
should reduce data-stall cycles on top of the current instruction-stall win
without adding IF routing, predictor state, extra retirement width, or a
MEM-to-EX load-data bypass.

title: Two-Word Line I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor result is the registered lookahead instruction
replay predictor. It kept the large cycle win of the earlier stall-only
replay cache, reducing CoreMark cycles from 4,226,169 to 3,459,314, while
moving the tag/data lookup off the same-cycle IF decision path enough to
reach 472.96 fitness. The predictor experiments after BTFNT and direct JAL
show the useful signal clearly: hiding random imem stalls pays much more
than adding another branch-direction table or return predictor.

The remaining replay loss is likely capacity conflict, not replay timing.
The 64-entry replay attempts gave up many cycles versus the 256-entry
version, so hot text footprint and aliasing matter. Simply doubling a
full-tag word table risks growing the registered lookup mux and tag storage
in the IF stage. A better predictor-shaped next step is to pack adjacent
instruction words under one line tag: it increases effective replay
capacity for sequential CoreMark code while amortizing the full PC tag over
two instructions and preserving the current registered-candidate timing
model.
hypothesis:
Replace the current one-word-per-entry replay table in `if_stage` with a
two-word direct-mapped replay-line predictor. Use a line index such as
`pc[10:3]`, a full line tag for the upper PC bits, two per-word valid bits,
and two 32-bit instruction slots selected by `pc[2]`. On every live
`io_imemReady=1` fetch, keep using `io_imemData` exactly as today and
refresh only the selected word within that line. On `io_imemReady=0`, the
current cycle still consumes only the registered lookahead candidate; a hit
requires the registered line tag to match the current PC line and the
selected word valid bit to be set.

This keeps the zero-wait and current-cycle IF path structurally equivalent
to the present best design: live imem wins whenever ready, replay data is
used only from a previously registered candidate, and the existing static
backward-branch plus direct-JAL predictor runs from the selected instruction
word. Redirects and reset still flush only the in-flight IF payload and
recompute the registered candidate for the next fetch PC; immutable replay
contents only need reset invalidation. Full line tags and per-word valid
bits preserve correctness under arbitrary PC values, so an alias can only
miss, never inject the wrong instruction. The expected gain is a modest
cycle reduction from fewer replay conflicts with lower Fmax risk than a
larger full-tag word table or any new branch-direction update path.

changes.description:

word entries to a direct-mapped two-word line organization. Store one
full line tag, two valid bits, and two 32-bit instruction words per
line; fill only the live-fetched word on `imem_ready`, use live imem
data on ready cycles, and use the selected registered replay word only
on an external imem stall with matching line tag and valid word bit.
Preserve the existing `fetch_ready` semantics, PC redirect priority,
same-PC fill bypass, static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction, and
no combinational table read on the current IF decision path.

Update only IF/ID prediction comments to describe that replayed

implementation_notes:
## Two-Word Line I-Fetch Replay Predictor

### What changed

- Replaced `if_stage`'s 256-entry one-word replay table with a 256-line direct-mapped replay-line table.
- Each replay line now uses index `pc[10:3]`, upper-PC tag `pc[31:11]`, two per-word valid bits, and two 32-bit instruction slots selected by `pc[2]`.
- Live `imem_ready` fetches still feed IF immediately and update only the selected word in the replay line. If the line tag changes, the other word is invalidated; if the tag matches, the other word's valid/data state is preserved.
- The IF stall replay path still uses only a registered lookahead candidate. The registered candidate now holds the full line address `pc[31:3]`, both valid bits, and both instruction words; a replay hit requires the candidate line to match the current PC line and the selected word valid bit to be set.
- Replay fills and hits are gated to word-aligned PCs so the two-word line offset never aliases a hypothetical misaligned fetch onto an aligned instruction word.
- Preserved live-imem priority, redirect priority over stalls, same-line/same-PC fill bypass into the next registered candidate, and static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction from the selected live or replayed instruction word.
- Updated `core_pkg.sv` IF/ID prediction comments to describe the registered two-word line replay predictor. No bundle widths, RVFI fields, architectural state, or top-level IO changed.

### Deviations

- The implementation keeps 256 direct-mapped entries, now interpreted as 256 two-word lines. This follows the hypothesis' `pc[10:3]` line-index suggestion and increases effective replay data capacity to 512 instruction words while amortizing each line tag across two words.
- The registered fill bypass merges a live fetched word into the candidate whenever the next lookup is for the same two-word line, not only when the exact PC matches. This is a conservative extension of the existing same-PC bypass and does not add a current-cycle table lookup to the IF decision path.

### Concerns

- No known functional concerns. The current-cycle IF decision still depends only on `imem_ready`, the registered candidate line, the current PC alignment, and the current PC word select.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the worktree was blocked by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared non-writable tree and the script refreshes `cores/auto-arch-researcher`.

title: Elide Replay Candidate PC Compare
motivation:
The current best result is dominated by the registered lookahead instruction
replay predictor. Compressing the replay table tags improved Fmax without
changing CoreMark cycles, which shows the replay-hit/fetch-ready path is now
one of the most timing-sensitive structures left in the design.

The IF stage still carries a full 32-bit `replay_cand_pc_q` register and
compares it against the current `pc` every cycle before a replay candidate
can satisfy an external imem stall. That guard is logically redundant with
the current lookahead update discipline: on every clock, the replay candidate
is computed for exactly the PC that will be held or loaded into the PC
register on the next cycle. Redirect loads both PC and candidate from the
redirect target, a non-stalled fetch loads both from `next_pc`, and a stalled
fetch recomputes the candidate for the held PC.

Removing this full-width equality check is a narrow micro-optimization in the
same proven area as the current best. It should reduce IF-side compare
fanout, remove 32 candidate-PC flops, and shorten the `fetch_ready` decision
path without changing replay hit rate, branch/JAL prediction policy,
pipeline timing, memory behavior, RVFI reporting, or CoreMark cycle count.
hypothesis:
Replace `replay_current_hit = replay_cand_valid_q && (replay_cand_pc_q == pc)`
with a candidate-valid-only hit signal, and remove `replay_cand_pc_q`
entirely. The invariant to preserve is that `replay_cand_valid_q` and
`replay_cand_instr_q` always describe the current fetch PC at the start of a
cycle:

- reset clears the candidate valid bit;
- EX redirect updates both `pc` and the next registered candidate from
  `redirect_target`;
- normal non-stalled fetch updates both from the same `next_pc`;
- stalled fetch holds `pc` and recomputes the candidate for that held `pc`;
- live `io_imemReady` still takes priority over replay data, and fill bypass
  still supplies a just-fetched word when the next candidate lookup aliases
  the just-filled replay entry.

On an external imem stall, `fetch_ready` therefore becomes
`replay_cand_valid_q` instead of a full 32-bit PC equality plus valid check.
Replay misses keep the existing PC-hold and IF-bubble behavior. The visible
instruction stream, predicted_taken/predicted_target metadata, EX
resolved-next-PC recovery, strict RVFI order, channel-0-only retirement, and
tied-off channel 1 remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove the `replay_cand_pc_q` register and the current-PC equality from
`replay_current_hit`. Treat the registered replay candidate valid bit as
the sole replay-hit guard because the candidate is already recomputed for
the next-cycle PC on every redirect, stall, and normal PC advance. Keep
live-imem priority, replay fill/update behavior, fill bypass, reset valid
clearing, static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction, and the existing
`fetch_ready` contract otherwise unchanged.

Clarify the IF/ID replay comments to state that the registered lookahead
candidate is maintained in lockstep with the fetch PC, so its valid bit
is sufficient to indicate a replay hit during external imem stalls. Do
not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality defaults,
RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Removed `replay_cand_pc_q` from `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Changed `replay_current_hit` to use `replay_cand_valid_q` directly, so `fetch_ready` is `imem_ready || replay_cand_valid_q`.
- Kept live imem priority over replay data and kept the replay fill bypass path unchanged.
- Narrowed the internal replay lookup PC signal to `[31:2]` because the low fetch-address bits are no longer consumed after removing the candidate-PC equality check.
- Updated IF replay comments in `rtl/if_stage.sv` and `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that the registered candidate is maintained in lockstep with the fetch PC.

## Deviations

- No architectural or interface deviations from the hypothesis.
- The only implementation detail beyond the hypothesis text is narrowing the replay lookup source from a full 32-bit signal to word-address bits to avoid an unused-signal lint warning.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns. The optimization relies on the existing IF update discipline: reset clears candidate valid, redirects load the target candidate, normal fetch advances candidate with `next_pc`, and stalls recompute for the held PC.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the repository tree could not stage files because this sandbox rejects writes through the symlinked/protected `formal/riscv-formal` tree (`rm ... Operation not permitted` before checks were generated).
- Ran the same formal script from a temporary copy with the `riscv-formal` symlink dereferenced into `/tmp` and renamed to a writable `formal/rf` path. Result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Registered MEM Load-Replay Candidate
motivation:
The current best core gets its largest IPC gain from registered lookahead
instruction replay: repeated hot instructions can be supplied during random
imem stalls without putting a same-cycle replay-table lookup on the IF
decision path. The analogous remaining structural loss is on the data side.
Loads still freeze the whole in-order pipeline whenever io_dmemReady is low,
even when the same normal-RAM word was recently returned by dmem.

The earlier MEM-stage load replay experiment showed the opportunity: it cut
CoreMark cycles, but the combinational replay-table lookup, tag compare, and
store merge logic in MEM lowered Fmax enough to miss the current best. The
promising structural angle is to keep the replay behavior while moving the
lookup off the MEM critical path, just as the successful IF replay predictor
did for instruction stalls.
hypothesis:
Add a small normal-RAM load replay table with a registered candidate for the
instruction entering MEM. When EX computes an effective address for a legal
load, use that address to look up the replay table one cycle early and latch a
candidate {hit, raw_word, tag/address} alongside the EX/MEM payload. In the
following MEM cycle, if io_dmemReady is low and the candidate still matches a
valid non-trapping normal-RAM load, complete the load from the registered raw
word: suppress the live dmem read, use the cached word for sign/zero
extension and RVFI mem_rdata, and do not assert mem_wait. If the candidate
misses, or if dmem is ready, preserve the current live-memory behavior.

Replay remains conservative. It is disabled while the pending store slot is
valid, for MMIO/marker/UART addresses, for misaligned or trapping memory
operations, and for stores. Accepted normal-RAM loads refresh the replay
table from live io_dmemRData. Normal-RAM stores either byte-merge-update or
invalidate a matching replay entry before the store retires or enqueues into
the pending store slot, so a replayed load cannot observe stale bytes.

This is a structural latency-hiding change, not a load-to-EX bypass: cached
data is used only at the MEM retirement boundary, and dependent younger
instructions still rely on the existing in-order load-use behavior. The
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 contract, channel-0-only retirement convention,
strict rvfi_order sequence, registered IF replay predictor, divider
interlock, branch prediction, and pending-store ordering all remain
unchanged. The expected win is to recover much of the prior load-replay cycle
reduction with less Fmax loss because MEM sees only a registered hit/data
candidate instead of a same-cycle table read.

changes.description:
Add a small direct-mapped normal-RAM replay table with valid, tag, and
raw-word data state. Provide a one-cycle-early lookup interface that
latches a replay candidate for the load entering MEM, refresh entries
from accepted live dmem load responses, and invalidate or byte-merge
matching entries on legal normal-RAM stores. Reset clears only valid and
candidate-valid state.

Expose the effective address and replay-eligible metadata for the
instruction being launched into EX/MEM so the load replay table can
register its lookup candidate one cycle before MEM uses it. Do not change
ALU results, forwarding, branch/JAL prediction repair, divider launch,
or RVFI operand metadata.

Consume the registered replay candidate during load handling. Select
cached raw word data only when dmem_ready is low, the current access is a
valid legal normal-RAM load, no pending store slot owns the port, and the
registered candidate matches. On a hit, gate off dmem_ren, clear
mem_wait, and drive load extraction plus RVFI mem_rdata from the cached
word. Keep replay misses and all nonbufferable accesses on the existing
wait path, and update or invalidate replay entries for stores.

Wire the one-cycle-early replay lookup sideband from EX to the replay
table and the registered replay candidate into MEM, while preserving the
existing registered IF replay wiring, pending-store-slot dmem arbitration,
top-level core IO shape, RVFI channel layout, and channel-1 tie-off.

Add only compact helper typedefs or comments if useful for carrying the
registered load-replay candidate and eligibility metadata. Do not change
architectural state semantics, decoder legality defaults, RVFI field
widths, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- Added `rtl/load_replay.sv`, a 16-entry direct-mapped normal-RAM load replay table with reset-cleared valid state and a registered MEM-stage replay candidate.
- Added `load_replay_cand_t` in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to carry `{valid, word-aligned address, raw word}` from the replay table to MEM.
- Extended `rtl/ex_stage.sv` to expose a one-cycle-early effective-address lookup sideband for valid, legal, aligned normal-RAM loads.
- Extended `rtl/mem_stage.sv` to consume the registered candidate only when `io_dmemReady` is low, the current instruction is still a valid legal normal-RAM load, and the pending store slot is not valid. Replay hits suppress the live dmem read, clear `mem_wait`, and drive both load extraction and RVFI `mem_rdata` from the cached raw word.
- Wired the replay table through `rtl/core.sv` while leaving top-level IO, RVFI channel layout, channel-1 tie-off, IF replay, divider interlock, branch prediction, and store-slot dmem arbitration unchanged.

## Deviations

- Store handling invalidates a matching replay entry instead of byte-merge-updating it. This is conservative and prevents stale replayed bytes while keeping the store-side replay-table update path smaller.
- The exact in-tree `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a sandbox-protected directory; it failed immediately with `Operation not permitted` while removing staged `.sv` files. I mirrored `rtl/` and `formal/` into `/tmp/codex-formal-load-replay-36775` with a writable `riscv-formal/cores` directory and ran the same script there.

## Concerns

- The replay table is intentionally small and direct-mapped; it may recover less IPC than a larger or merge-updating table.
- The lookup is off the MEM wait path, but it still adds an EX effective-address-to-candidate-register setup path. The FPGA fitness run will decide whether that is cheaper than the old same-cycle MEM replay lookup.

## Local Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: in-place run blocked by sandbox permissions before proofs began.
- `/tmp` mirror `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Phase-Aligned Replay Candidate
motivation:
The current best result comes from the compressed, resetless registered
lookahead instruction replay predictor. It keeps the large random-iStall
cycle win of the 256-entry replay table while avoiding a same-cycle table
read in IF. The predictor experiments since then show that adding more
branch-direction or return-target state is not attractive: those changes
saved a few cycles but lost more Fmax than they recovered.

The remaining predictor timing cost is inside the replay fast decision
itself. `if_stage` still carries a full 32-bit `replay_cand_pc_q` and gates
replay hits with `replay_cand_pc_q == pc` before asserting `fetch_ready`.
That compare is on the current IF stall/PC-enable path even though the
candidate is already generated from the exact same redirect/stall/next-PC
selection that updates the PC register. After a clock edge, the registered
replay candidate is phase-aligned with the current PC by construction.

Removing the redundant current-PC equality check is a narrower predictor
improvement than changing table capacity or direction policy. It preserves
the current replay hit rate and CoreMark cycle count while shortening the
fetch-ready path and dropping the 32-bit candidate-PC register.
hypothesis:
Replace the full-PC-guarded replay candidate with a phase-aligned valid
candidate. In `if_stage`, compute `replay_lookup_pc` from the same priority
mux that determines the next value of `pc`: EX redirect target first, then
predicted/fallthrough next PC when fetch is not stalled, otherwise the held
current PC. Use that lookup to register only `{valid, instr}` for the next
cycle. Because the PC register loads the same selected address on the same
clock edge, `replay_cand_valid_q` means the cached instruction candidate is
for the current fetch PC on the following cycle.

With that invariant, drive `fetch_ready = io_imemReady || replay_cand_valid_q`
and select `replay_cand_instr_q` on an external imem stall whenever the
candidate valid bit is set. Remove `replay_cand_pc_q` and the 32-bit
equality check from the current-cycle IF path. The replay table itself keeps
the current 256-entry organization, compressed high-PC tags, resetless
tag/instruction payloads, live-imem-first fill policy, and same-index fill
bypass. Reset still clears the replay valid bits and candidate valid state;
redirects and held-PC cycles recompute the candidate from the same address
the PC will hold or load.

This should be bit-equivalent for all architecturally reachable traces:
replay contents are still selected only after a valid table lookup for the
selected next PC, live imem data still wins whenever ready, replay misses
still hold the PC and inject the existing IF bubble, and EX still repairs
branch/JAL prediction mistakes through resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery.
The top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 contract, channel-0 retirement order, pending
store slot, divider interlock, decoder legality rules, and static
backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction policy remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove `replay_cand_pc_q` and the full 32-bit
`replay_cand_pc_q == pc` replay-hit guard. Register the replay
candidate from the same selected next-PC value that the PC register will
hold after the clock edge, so the candidate valid bit is phase-aligned
with the current fetch PC. Drive effective `fetch_ready` from live imem
readiness or this phase-aligned candidate valid bit, and preserve the
existing replay table size, compressed high tags, resetless payload
arrays, fill bypass, live-imem priority, redirect priority, and static
branch/JAL prediction behavior.

Update only IF/ID prediction comments to document that replay candidates
are registered for the same next-PC value loaded by the IF PC register,
so candidate validity is phase-aligned with the current fetch PC. Do not
change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality defaults,
RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` to remove the 32-bit `replay_cand_pc_q` register and the `replay_cand_pc_q == pc` replay-hit guard.
- `fetch_ready` is now driven by `imem_ready || replay_cand_valid_q`.
- During an external imem stall, IF now selects `replay_cand_instr_q` whenever `replay_cand_valid_q` is set.
- Kept the existing replay table organization: 256 direct-mapped entries, high-PC tags, resetless tag/instruction payloads, valid-bit reset, live-imem-first fill, and same-index fill bypass.
- Narrowed the replay lookup address storage used inside IF to `replay_lookup_pc_word[31:2]`, since the low two PC bits are architecturally zero for fetch and no longer feed a full-PC equality check.
- Updated the IF/ID comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that replay candidates are registered for the same next-PC value loaded by IF's PC register.

## Deviations

- No architectural or bundle-width changes were made.
- No changes were made outside `rtl/` except this notes file.
- The normal `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation could not stage files in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable worktree. I ran the same script from a dereferenced `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-r14s2` copy containing the current `rtl/` instead.

## Concerns

- The change relies on the existing invariant that `replay_lookup_pc_word` is selected from the same redirect/stall/next-PC priority used by the PC register update. That invariant is now directly reflected in the remaining IF logic.
- No known functional concerns from lint or formal.

## Local Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked in-place by sandbox permissions on the symlinked shared `formal/riscv-formal` staging directory before checks ran.
- Equivalent `/tmp` formal run using a copied `formal/` tree and current `rtl/`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Valid-Only Pipeline Payload Reset
motivation:
The current best design is dominated by timing-only wins that do not change
CoreMark cycles: the segmented RVFI order counter and the compressed
resetless IF replay metadata both improved Fmax by removing unnecessary
carry, compare, and reset pressure from always-present logic. The remaining
pipeline registers still use broad `<= '0` clears on reset, flush, and
injected bubbles, even though most payload bits are architecturally
irrelevant whenever the stage valid bit is zero.

Those full-bundle clears add synchronous reset/clear muxes and reset fanout
to wide ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB, divide-side metadata, and replay-candidate
payload registers. The data fields include PCs, instructions, operands,
immediates, memory words, and RVFI metadata; only a small subset of valid
bits and side-effect controls must be known zero to prevent writes,
memory requests, redirects, or incorrect forwarding. A valid-gated payload
cleanup is a narrow micro-optimization in the same class as the measured
replay tag reset elision: preserve the exact instruction stream and
retirement behavior, but remove needless reset/clear logic from hot
pipeline boundaries.
hypothesis:
Convert the main pipeline and sidecar payload registers to valid-only or
side-effect-only reset discipline. Reset, redirect flushes, and long-op
bubbles should clear the stage `valid` bit and any control enables that can
cause an architectural side effect (`reg_write`, `mem_read`, `mem_write`,
branch/jump redirect enables, and forwarding-visible write enables). The
wide payload fields behind those valid bits may remain resetless and do not
need to be overwritten with zero on bubbles.

Consumers must be made explicitly valid-aware wherever they are not already:
load-use detection should see `id_ex.valid && id_ex.ctrl.mem_read`;
EX/MEM forwarding should require a valid producer; MEM/WB forwarding must
preserve the existing held-load forwarding behavior during `hold_wb` while
still being safe immediately after reset. MEM-stage dmem requests, WB
writes, redirects, and RVFI retirement already key off valid and should keep
that discipline. Channel 1 remains tied off, and channel 0 RVFI fields are
only meaningful when `io_rvfi_valid_0` is asserted.

The visible 64-bit RVFI order sequence, `pc_wdata`, trap behavior, divider
interlock, IF replay predictor, pending-store slot, memory ordering, and
CoreMark cycle count should remain bit-identical. The expected gain is a
modest Fmax improvement from removing reset/clear muxes and reset routing on
several hundred nonarchitectural payload flops without adding new datapath
work or predictor state.

changes.description:
Change reset and flush handling for the ID/EX register so only
`valid` and side-effecting control bits are forced to a safe bubble.
Leave operand, PC, instruction, immediate, address, and prediction
payload fields resetless behind the cleared valid bit. Preserve decoder
illegal-default behavior and the existing capture path when the stage is
not stalled.

Apply the same valid-only clear discipline to the EX/MEM register and
divide metadata. Reset or bubble cycles should clear `valid` and any
forwarding-visible or memory/writeback side-effect controls, but should
not zero wide payload fields that are ignored while invalid. Keep the
divider launch/done protocol, branch/JAL/JALR repair logic, and RVFI
operand metadata semantics unchanged.

Convert MEM/WB reset to clear only `valid` and the minimal control
fields needed for safe post-reset forwarding/writeback behavior. Keep
the existing `hold_wb` behavior that clears valid while retaining load
metadata for forwarding during a dmem stall. Do not alter load
extraction, store-slot enqueue, dmem request gating, misalignment traps,
or RVFI memory fields for valid retirements.

Make forwarding producer enables explicitly valid-aware for EX/MEM, and
preserve the current MEM/WB held-load forwarding case with a safe
post-reset enable. This prevents resetless invalid payloads from being
selected while keeping all existing true bypass behavior.

Gate hazard and forwarding inputs with the appropriate stage-valid or
held-forwarding predicates so resetless payload fields cannot influence
stalls or operand selection. Preserve the top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 port
layout, strict channel-0 order counter, pending-store-slot arbitration,
IF replay wiring, and channel-1 tie-off.

Update pipeline-bundle comments to document that many payload fields are
resetless and are only architecturally meaningful when the associated
valid bit and side-effect controls are asserted. Do not change bundle
widths, RVFI fields, decoder legality defaults, architectural state, or
the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Converted ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB, divider side metadata, IF replay-candidate payload, and pending-store-slot payload reset handling toward valid-only discipline.
- Reset, redirect/bubble, and invalid-capture paths now clear `valid` plus side-effecting controls (`reg_write`, `mem_read`, `mem_write`, branch/jump redirect controls) while leaving wide payload fields resetless.
- Gated load-use detection with `id_ex.valid && id_ex.ctrl.mem_read`.
- Made EX/MEM forwarding valid-aware. MEM/WB forwarding keeps the existing held-writeback behavior by treating `mem_wb.ctrl.reg_write` as a forwarding-visible enable; reset and invalid captures now clear it, while `hold_wb` intentionally retains it.
- Updated bundle comments to document resetless payload validity rules.

## Deviations

- Also applied the same payload reset elision to `div_unit.sv`, `if_stage.sv` replay-candidate payload fields, and `store_slot.sv` payload fields. These are behind explicit valid/state bits and match the hypothesis intent even though they were not all in the advisory file list.
- Did not add any new architectural state, change bundle widths, alter top-level IO, or change the RVFI NRET=2 channel contract.

## Concerns

- Channel-0 RVFI payload fields may carry stale/resetless values when `io_rvfi_valid_0` is low. This is intentional; visible RVFI retirements remain valid-gated.
- The direct in-tree `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation is blocked in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected path and the script cannot refresh `cores/auto-arch-researcher/*.sv` there.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass when run from a disposable `/tmp` copy of the same project inputs; final tally was `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- No RTL formal counterexamples were observed, so no formal-driven fix attempts were needed.

title: Two-Entry Pending Store Slot
motivation:
The current best core combines the proven structural pieces: cold DIV/REM is
out of the universal EX path, the one-deep pending-store slot hides some
normal-RAM store backpressure, and the registered instruction replay table
removes most random iStall loss. The remaining structural stalls are now
concentrated on the data side. A single pending-store entry lets one stalled
RAM store retire, but the next store still freezes the whole pipeline while
the older slot drains, even though both stores already have their address,
replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI memory metadata available in MEM.

A two-entry store buffer was attempted before the large post-DIV and replay
timing wins and failed placement on a much larger design. The current RTL is
substantially smaller and already has the timing-local one-entry direct fast
path, so revisiting only a second pending-store slot is the cleanest
structural bet: it targets store bursts under the 22% random dStall model
without adding load data to EX, changing the branch predictor, widening
retirement, or touching the IF replay timing path.
hypothesis:
Extend the pending-store retirement slot into a two-entry oldest-first FIFO
for legal, aligned normal-RAM stores only. When the FIFO is empty and
`io_dmemReady` is high, keep the existing direct ready-store path unchanged:
the live MEM-stage store drives the external dmem port and retires normally.
When a bufferable store cannot use the port because `io_dmemReady` is low or
because an older pending store already owns the port, enqueue the store into
any free FIFO entry, retire it once through MEM/WB with its RVFI memory
fields, and allow younger non-memory instructions to continue.

The FIFO drains oldest-first onto the single external dmem port. While any
pending store is valid, younger loads, younger stores that cannot enqueue,
and all MMIO/marker/UART stores must wait behind the buffered store stream.
This preserves in-order memory visibility and keeps start/stop marker timing
exact. Misaligned, illegal, trapping, or non-normal-RAM stores never enter the
FIFO and keep the current wait-for-handshake behavior. If both entries are
full, the MEM stage asserts the existing precise `mem_wait` interlock.

This should recover store-burst stall cycles that the one-entry slot cannot
absorb while keeping the hot zero-wait memory path close to the current best
RTL. The top-level core IO, RVFI NRET=2 port set, channel-0-only single
retirement convention, strict `rvfi_order` sequence, divider interlock,
registered IF replay predictor, and decoder legality invariants remain
unchanged.

changes.description:
Replace the one-entry pending-store slot with a two-entry oldest-first
FIFO. Expose valid/nonempty, full, enqueue-ready, and oldest drain
signals. Support simultaneous drain and enqueue so a ready drain can
free capacity for a younger store in the same cycle. Each entry holds
only the byte address, replicated write data, and byte mask; RVFI
metadata still retires through the normal MEM/WB path on enqueue.

Treat legal aligned normal-RAM stores as bufferable whenever the FIFO has
space and the live dmem port is unavailable because `io_dmemReady` is low
or an older pending store is draining. Keep the direct ready-store path
unchanged when the FIFO is empty and `io_dmemReady` is high. Assert
`mem_wait` for loads and nonbufferable/MMIO stores behind any valid
pending store, and for bufferable stores only when the FIFO is full.
Preserve misalignment traps, dmem gating, RVFI `mem_addr`, `mem_wmask`,
and `mem_wdata` semantics.

Wire the two-entry store FIFO into the existing top-level dmem
arbitration. Give the FIFO's oldest entry priority on the external port
while valid, route live MEM-stage requests directly only when the FIFO is
empty, and pass FIFO fullness/capacity back to MEM. Preserve the core
module IO shape, registered IF replay wiring, RVFI channel layout, strict
channel-0 order counter behavior, and tied-off channel 1.

Keep consuming the MEM-stage `mem_wait` interlock, but update comments
and any naming if needed to reflect that a store can retire into either
pending-store FIFO entry while younger non-memory work continues. Do not
change load-use, imem replay readiness, EX long-operation, redirect, or
MEM/WB hold semantics.

title: Registered I-Fetch Replay Victim Predictor
motivation:
The current best design is driven by the registered lookahead instruction
replay predictor with compressed, resetless tags. That predictor keeps the
large random-iStall cycle reduction of the 256-entry replay table while
avoiding a same-cycle table read on the IF decision path. The experiment log
also shows the boundary clearly: smaller replay tables gave up too much hit
rate, two-word replay lines recovered only a little cycle count while losing
Fmax, and branch-direction or return predictors have consistently lost more
clock than their control-flow savings recovered.

The remaining replay misses are therefore most likely direct-mapped conflict
misses in CoreMark's hot text footprint, not a lack of branch-direction
sophistication. A tiny victim predictor behind the existing 256-entry replay
table targets those conflicts without widening the main table, adding a new
branch update path, or putting a combinational lookup back onto current-cycle
fetch_ready.
hypothesis:
Keep the current 256-entry registered lookahead replay table, compressed
high-PC tags, resetless replay payload arrays, live-imem-first policy, and
registered replay candidate semantics. Add a small fully-associative victim
replay buffer, for example four entries, that stores recently evicted replay
words as `{valid, word_pc_tag, instr}`. The victim entries are looked up only
while computing the registered lookahead candidate for the next fetch PC; the
current IF cycle still selects only between live `io_imemData` and the
previously registered replay candidate.

On a live `io_imemReady=1` fetch, refresh the direct-mapped replay entry for
the current PC exactly as today. If that direct entry is valid for a different
high tag, move the evicted word into the victim buffer using a simple
round-robin or age pointer. If the live fetch PC already exists in the victim
buffer, invalidate that victim copy after refreshing the direct entry so the
direct table remains authoritative. On lookahead lookup, register a replay
candidate on either a direct-table hit or a victim-buffer hit, with direct hit
priority. Replay misses still hold the PC and inject the existing IF bubble
when external imem is not ready.

This should recover hot-loop replay misses caused by a few PCs aliasing in
the direct-mapped table while keeping the current-cycle IF path nearly
unchanged: `fetch_ready` is still driven from live imem readiness or the
registered candidate hit, branch/JAL prediction still runs from the selected
live or registered replay word, and EX still repairs any control prediction
mistake through resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery. Reset clears only valid
bits and candidate state; victim tags/instruction payloads can remain
resetless behind valid bits. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, strict
channel-0 order, tied-off channel 1, pending-store behavior, divider
interlock, and decoder legality rules remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add a four-entry fully-associative victim replay buffer behind the
existing 256-entry registered lookahead replay table. Store evicted
replay words with a valid bit, full word-PC tag, and instruction data;
clear only valid bits on reset. During registered lookahead generation,
choose a direct-table hit first and a victim hit second, then register
the selected candidate exactly as the current replay path does. On live
imem fills, move displaced valid direct entries into the victim buffer,
invalidate duplicate victim entries for the just-refreshed PC, and keep
live imem data as the selected instruction whenever `io_imemReady` is
high. Preserve compressed direct tags, resetless payload arrays,
same-index fill bypass, redirect priority, static backward-branch and
direct-JAL prediction, and the existing `fetch_ready` contract.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to document that a replayed instruction
may come from the direct-mapped table or from a small registered-lookahead
victim predictor. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state,
decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes Made

- Added a four-entry fully-associative victim replay buffer in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Victim entries store `{valid, pc[31:2], instr}` and are searched only for the registered lookahead replay candidate.
- Kept live imem priority and the existing current-cycle IF contract: `fetch_ready` is still driven only by `imem_ready` or a previously registered replay candidate hit.
- Preserved direct-table priority over victim hits, including same-index live fill bypass priority.
- On live imem fills, the direct-mapped replay table is refreshed as before. A valid displaced direct entry with a different compressed high tag is inserted into the victim buffer through a round-robin replacement pointer.
- On live imem fills, matching victim entries for the just-refreshed PC are invalidated so the direct table remains authoritative.
- Reset clears replay valid bits, victim valid bits, replacement pointer, and candidate state only; direct/victim tag and instruction payload arrays remain resetless behind valid bits.
- Updated the IF/ID replay comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that replayed words may come from either the direct table or the victim buffer.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The victim size is four entries and uses simple round-robin replacement.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not run directly in the worktree because the sandbox blocked the script's staging cleanup under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`. I ran the same unmodified script from a writable `/tmp/aa-formal-run-r15s2` mirror containing the current `rtl/` files and symlinked read-only riscv-formal resources.

## Concerns

- Yosys reports that the four-entry victim arrays are replaced with lists of registers during formal elaboration. That is expected for the small fully-associative structure and is not a functional issue.
- The victim lookup adds four tag compares to the registered lookahead path, not the current-cycle `fetch_ready` path. Fmax impact should be limited, but FPGA P&R is still the real arbiter.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass via writable temp mirror, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Resetless Replay Candidate Payloads
motivation:
The current best result is built around the registered lookahead instruction
replay predictor. The strongest recent micro-opt win came from compressing
replay tags and making replay table payload arrays resetless behind valid
bits; broader attempts to remove the replay candidate PC guard or apply
valid-only reset discipline across the whole pipeline regressed. That points
to a narrower follow-up in the same proven area.

`if_stage` still synchronously resets the registered replay candidate PC and
instruction payload to `RESET_PC` and `NOP`, even though those fields are
consumed only when `replay_cand_valid_q` is asserted and the existing full-PC
guard matches the current fetch PC. Reset muxes and reset fanout on these
64 payload bits sit next to the IF replay/fetch-ready path that now matters
most for Fmax. Clearing only the candidate valid bit should preserve the
current replay hit rate and all visible behavior while trimming a small but
timing-relevant reset structure.
hypothesis:
Make the registered replay candidate payload resetless while keeping the
current correctness guard intact. On reset, clear `replay_cand_valid_q` and
the replay table valid bits exactly as today, but do not assign reset values
to `replay_cand_pc_q` or `replay_cand_instr_q`. The replay candidate remains
selectable only when `replay_cand_valid_q && (replay_cand_pc_q == pc)`, so
resetless PC/instruction payload bits cannot affect fetch, decode, RVFI, or
branch/JAL prediction until they have been written by a real lookahead
lookup or fill-bypass update.

This intentionally does not repeat the prior candidate-PC-compare removal:
the full current-PC equality check stays in place, live `io_imemData` still
has priority whenever `io_imemReady` is high, replay misses still create the
same PC-hold and IF bubble, and the 256-entry compressed-tag replay table,
static backward-branch/direct-JAL predictor, redirect priority, divider
interlock, pending-store slot, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement,
and strict `rvfi_order` sequence remain unchanged. CoreMark cycles should be
bit-identical; the expected gain is a modest median Fmax improvement from
removing reset muxing and reset routing on the replay candidate payload
registers in the timing-sensitive IF replay block.

changes.description:
Change the replay-candidate reset block so reset clears only
`replay_cand_valid_q` and the replay table valid bits. Leave
`replay_cand_pc_q` and `replay_cand_instr_q` resetless behind the
existing candidate-valid and full-PC match guard. Preserve live-imem
priority, replay fill/update behavior, fill bypass, compressed replay
tags, static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction, redirect priority,
and the current `fetch_ready` contract.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to document that the registered replay
candidate payload is resetless behind its valid bit and current-PC guard.
Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality
defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` so reset clears only `replay_cand_valid_q` and the `replay_valid` table bits.
- Moved replay payload updates into a separate resetless clocked block. This leaves `replay_cand_pc_q`, `replay_cand_instr_q`, `replay_tag_hi`, and `replay_instr` behind their existing valid-bit protection.
- Preserved the existing live-imem priority, fill-bypass behavior, compressed replay tags, and full current-PC replay candidate guard.
- Updated the IF/ID replay comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document the resetless replay-candidate payload.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The implementation uses a separate resetless payload block so synthesis does not need reset fanout or reset-driven hold muxing on the candidate PC/instruction payload flops.

## Concerns

- No known correctness concerns. During reset, replay payload contents may be arbitrary or continue updating, but replay selection remains blocked by `replay_cand_valid_q == 0` and the replay table valid bits are cleared.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the worktree was blocked before checks ran because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink outside the writable sandbox and the script's staging cleanup uses `rm -f` there.
- To run the same gate without modifying the protected formal tree, I copied the formal harness to `/tmp/formal-run.8xxSkF`, symlinked its `rtl/` back to this worktree, and ran the unmodified copied `run_all.sh` there.
- Temporary-harness formal result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Same-Cycle Store Slot Refill
motivation:
The current best core already has the two structural wins that matter most:
registered instruction replay hides many random iStall cycles, and the
one-deep pending-store slot lets a stalled normal-RAM store retire before
the external dmem write is accepted. The remaining store-side bottleneck is
narrower than a full second buffer entry. When the pending slot is valid, the
MEM stage currently treats every younger memory operation as blocked even on
the exact cycle where `io_dmemReady` will drain the older buffered store.

That leaves a small but avoidable bubble for bursts of normal-RAM stores:
the older store uses the physical dmem port, while the younger store already
has its address, replicated data, byte mask, and RVFI-visible memory fields
available in MEM and could refill the just-drained slot. A previous
two-entry FIFO targeted the same store-burst pressure but failed placement.
The existing `store_slot` state machine already supports simultaneous drain
and enqueue, so the lower-risk structural angle is to expose that fast refill
case without adding another entry or touching the IF replay path.
hypothesis:
Allow a legal, aligned normal-RAM store in MEM to enqueue into the pending
store slot on the same cycle that an older slot entry drains
(`store_slot_valid && io_dmemReady`). The top-level dmem port still gives the
older slot entry priority for that cycle. The younger store retires once
through MEM/WB with its normal RVFI `mem_addr`, `mem_wmask`, and `mem_wdata`,
and the slot remains valid with the younger store's physical drain fields for
a later external write.

Keep all existing ordering constraints. If the slot is valid and the port is
not ready, younger memory operations still wait. Loads, MMIO/marker/UART
stores, misaligned stores, illegal/trapping accesses, and any non-normal-RAM
store still wait behind the slot and never enter it. If the slot is empty,
the current direct ready-store path and low-ready enqueue path remain
unchanged. Younger non-memory instructions continue to pass while a buffered
store drains, as they do today.

This should recover part of the store-burst stall cycles that motivated the
failed two-entry FIFO, but with almost no additional placement pressure:
only the MEM-stage enqueue/wait predicate changes, and the existing one-entry
slot already implements the simultaneous drain-plus-enqueue update. The
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 channel layout, strict channel-0 order sequence,
tied-off channel 1, registered IF replay predictor, divider interlock, branch
prediction, and decoder legality invariants remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Refine pending-store backpressure so a bufferable normal-RAM store can
enqueue when the existing slot is valid and `dmem_ready` is high, because
the older slot entry is draining on that same cycle. Preserve the direct
ready-store path when the slot is empty, the existing low-ready enqueue
path when the slot is empty and the port stalls, and full waits for
loads, nonbufferable stores, MMIO/marker/UART stores, traps, and memory
operations behind a non-draining valid slot.

Keep the one-entry slot state machine, but document and, if needed,
tighten the simultaneous drain-plus-enqueue behavior so a draining older
store can be replaced by a younger bufferable store in the same clock
edge. The slot still holds only physical drain fields; RVFI metadata
remains retired through MEM/WB on the enqueue cycle.

Preserve the current top-level dmem arbitration with pending-slot
priority while valid. Update comments or local naming only if needed to
make clear that `store_slot_valid && io_dmemReady` represents available
enqueue capacity after the older store drains, without changing the core
module IO, RVFI ports, IF replay wiring, or channel-1 tie-off.

title: Replay-Cached Next-PC Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor is the compressed, resetless registered
lookahead instruction replay table. It keeps the large random-iStall cycle
win of the 256-entry replay cache while avoiding a same-cycle table lookup
on the IF decision path. Later predictor experiments show that adding new
branch-direction tables, return predictors, victim entries, or wider replay
lines either saves very few cycles or loses more Fmax than it recovers.

The remaining promising predictor work is therefore inside the replay path
that already wins. On a replay hit, the IF stage currently feeds the cached
instruction word back through the same static branch/JAL decode and target
adders used for live imem data. That means an external imem stall served by
replay still has a flop-to-flop path from `replay_cand_instr_q` through
branch/JAL immediate reconstruction, target adders, the next-PC mux, and the
PC register. The replay table already knows the instruction for that PC from
an earlier live fetch, so its static next-PC prediction can be cached at fill
time and replayed as metadata instead of recomputed in the hot stall path.
hypothesis:
Extend each instruction replay-table entry with compact static next-PC
prediction metadata: a predicted-taken bit and a predicted target or
predicted next-PC value computed from the live `io_imemData` and current PC
when the entry is filled. The existing instruction word, compressed high-PC
tag, valid bit, live-imem-first policy, resetless payload arrays, registered
lookahead candidate, and same-index fill bypass remain in place.

When `io_imemReady=1`, IF still uses the live instruction word and the
current static backward-branch/direct-JAL predictor exactly as it does
today; this preserves the formal-ready path and keeps live imem authoritative.
The replay entry is refreshed with the live instruction plus the computed
metadata. When `io_imemReady=0` and the registered replay candidate hits the
current PC, IF should use the registered metadata to drive `predicted_taken`,
`predicted_target`, and the PC update, while still passing the cached
instruction word into decode. Replay misses keep the current PC-hold and
IF-bubble behavior.

This should be bit-equivalent for all replayed instructions because the
cached metadata is just the current static predictor result computed at the
previous live fetch of the same immutable instruction word. EX still checks
the carried prediction against the resolved next PC, so any branch outcome
mismatch is repaired exactly as today. The expected gain is a modest Fmax
improvement by removing branch/JAL immediate decode and target-add work from
the replay-hit PC update path, with no intended CoreMark cycle-count change
and no top-level IO, RVFI, divider, memory, or retirement-contract changes.

changes.description:
Add replay-table metadata arrays and registered candidate fields for the
static next-PC prediction associated with each cached instruction. Fill
them on live `io_imemReady` fetches using the same backward-branch and
direct-JAL prediction rule currently computed from `fetch_instr`. On a
replay hit during an external imem stall, bypass the instruction decode
and target adders for PC prediction by using the registered metadata to
drive `predicted_taken`, `predicted_target`, and `next_pc`. Keep live
imem cycles on the existing live decode path, preserve compressed replay
tags, resetless payload arrays, valid-bit reset, fill bypass, redirect
priority, `fetch_ready` semantics, and replay-miss stalls.

Clarify the IF/ID prediction comments to state that replayed
instructions may carry precomputed static next-PC metadata from the
registered replay predictor, while live imem instructions still compute
prediction metadata directly from the live word. Do not change bundle
widths, architectural state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields,
top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Extended the IF replay table with resetless static prediction metadata arrays:
  `replay_pred_taken` and `replay_pred_next_pc`.
- Added registered lookahead candidate fields for the replay metadata, including
  same-index fill bypass alongside the existing instruction bypass.
- Split IF static prediction so live imem cycles compute prediction from
  `imem_data` and `pc`, while replay-hit cycles select the registered metadata
  instead of decoding `replay_cand_instr_q` for branch/JAL immediates.
- Kept live imem authoritative: when `imem_ready` is high, IF uses the live
  instruction word and live-computed prediction metadata.
- Updated `core_pkg.sv` comments to describe live-computed versus replay-cached
  IF prediction metadata without changing bundle widths.

## Deviations

- The replay table stores predicted next PC rather than a separate target value.
  This matches the hypothesis allowance for either target or next-PC metadata and
  avoids recomputing the not-taken `pc+4` value on replay hits.
- The metadata is computed as the ungated static prediction at fill time. Runtime
  `flush`, `redirect`, and `stall` gating is still applied when metadata is used,
  preserving the previous IF behavior while avoiding stale false predictions from
  entries filled during a pipeline stall.

## Concerns

- The change adds 33 metadata bits per replay entry. This is intentional for the
  requested Fmax tradeoff and does not alter top-level IO, RVFI, decode legality,
  memory, divider, or retirement contracts.
- No known behavioral concerns. Replay misses still hold PC and bubble IF through
  the existing `fetch_ready` path.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree could not stage files because
  `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink outside the writable sandbox; the script
  failed before generating checks with `Operation not permitted` on the stale
  staged `.sv` cleanup.
- Equivalent local formal run from a writable `/tmp` copy of the project and
  riscv-formal framework: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: RTL-Only Hot/Cold ALU Opcode Split
motivation:
The current best core has already removed the cold DIV/REM arithmetic from
the universal EX path and gained another large timing win from trimming the
registered instruction replay metadata. The remaining micro-opt opportunity
should therefore avoid new queues, predictors, reset rewrites, or
multi-cycle units, and instead reshape logic that is already on the hot
one-cycle EX datapath.

`rtl/alu.sv` still uses a dense ALU operation encoding and a single broad
result case for the common RV32I operations, the single-cycle multiply
results, and dummy DIV/REM results that are now supplied by `div_unit.sv`.
The two previous hot/cold ALU hypotheses never produced architectural data
because their implementations were rejected for touching an off-limits test
helper, not because the RTL idea failed formal, cosim, or FPGA placement.
The source still has the same dense opcode/mux shape, so this remains the
cleanest unevaluated micro-opt angle.
hypothesis:
Re-encode the ALU operation constants so `alu_op[4]` is an explicit
M-extension discriminator. Keep every RV32I/simple operation at
`op[4]=0`, and move `MUL`, `MULH`, `MULHSU`, `MULHU`, `DIV`, `DIVU`,
`REM`, and `REMU` to `op[4]=1`, preferably `5'b10000` through
`5'b10111` in RV32M funct3 order.

Refactor `rtl/alu.sv` into two local result muxes. The hot simple mux covers
ADD/SUB, logic, compare, shift, and LUI only. The cold M mux covers the
existing multiply results and the existing dummy zero result for DIV/REM
opcodes handled by the iterative divider. Drive the final ALU output with a
single `op[4] ? m_out : simple_out` select.

This does not change any arithmetic primitive, instruction latency,
forwarding path, branch predictor, memory behavior, divider protocol,
top-level IO, RVFI field, retirement order, or CoreMark cycle count. The
RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS multiply substitutions stay in the cold M mux under the
new constants, while divide/remainder ALTOPS behavior remains in
`div_unit.sv`. The expected benefit is a modest median Fmax improvement from
reducing decode and result-mux depth on the common RV32I EX result path.

changes.description:
Reassign ALU_MUL through ALU_REMU to an `op[4]=1` encoding, preferably
contiguous values `5'b10000` through `5'b10111` matching RV32M funct3
order. Keep all ADD/SUB/logic/compare/shift/LUI operations at
`op[4]=0`. Do not change bundle widths, RVFI fields, architectural
state, decoder legality defaults, or the NRET=2 contract.

Split the single broad ALU result case into `simple_out` and `m_out`
sub-muxes. `simple_out` should cover only the RV32I hot path. `m_out`
should reuse the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU arithmetic and ALTOPS
formulas, while DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU continue to return zero because the
iterative divider supplies their real result. Select the final output
only with `op[4]`.

# Implementation Notes
