title: Tiny MRU Load Replay Latch
motivation:
The current best core already uses the two structural ideas that have paid
clearly: a one-deep pending-store slot for store-only dmem backpressure and
a registered instruction replay table for random iStall. The remaining
structural stalls are concentrated on normal-RAM loads. A load still freezes
the whole in-order pipeline whenever io_dmemReady is low, even if the same
aligned word was returned recently.

The prior 16-entry MEM-stage load replay buffer showed that this stall source
is real: it reduced CoreMark cycles on top of the instruction replay design.
It missed the current best because the same-cycle table lookup, tag compare,
and store-update logic dropped Fmax too much. A much smaller MRU replay latch
targets the same dmem-stall opportunity while keeping the MEM path close to
the current best design.
hypothesis:
Add a two-entry most-recently-used normal-RAM load replay latch in MEM. Each
entry stores a valid bit, an aligned word address tag, and the raw 32-bit word
returned by a previously accepted legal normal-RAM load. On io_dmemReady=1,
loads keep using live io_dmemRData exactly as today and update the MRU latch
with the aligned word and raw data. On io_dmemReady=0, a valid legal
normal-RAM load may complete from the latch if either entry matches the
aligned word address: suppress dmem_ren, use the cached raw word for
sign/zero extension and RVFI mem_rdata, and do not assert mem_wait.

Replay remains conservative. It is disabled while the pending store slot is
valid, for MMIO/marker/UART addresses, for misaligned or trapping accesses,
and for stores. Normal-RAM stores byte-merge any matching replay entry before
they retire or enqueue into the pending-store slot; if a simple merge is not
available for a corner case, invalidating the matching entry is acceptable.
Replay misses preserve the current dmem wait behavior exactly.

This is a structural latency-hiding change, not a MEM-to-EX bypass. Cached
data is consumed only at the MEM retirement boundary, dependent younger
instructions still use the existing load-use discipline, and the top-level IO,
RVFI NRET=2 channel layout, channel-0-only retirement order, divider
interlock, registered IF replay predictor, branch prediction, pending-store
ordering, and decoder legality invariants remain unchanged. The expected win
is a small cycle reduction from repeated normal-RAM loads during random
dmem stalls with much less Fmax cost than the earlier 16-entry load replay
table.

changes.description:
Add a tiny two-entry MRU replay latch for normal-RAM load words. Entries
hold valid, aligned word address tag, and raw 32-bit data. Provide lookup
hit/data for the current MEM load, update from accepted live load
responses, and byte-merge or invalidate matching entries on legal
normal-RAM stores. Reset clears only valid bits.

Integrate the MRU replay lookup into load handling. Select cached raw word
data only when dmem_ready is low, the current access is a valid legal
normal-RAM load, no pending store slot is valid, and the latch hits. On a
hit, gate off dmem_ren, clear mem_wait, and drive load extraction plus
RVFI mem_rdata from the cached word. Keep replay misses and all
nonbufferable accesses on the existing wait path.

Add only comments or compact helper typedefs if useful to document the
MRU load replay eligibility and raw-word metadata. Do not change
architectural state semantics, decoder legality defaults, RVFI field
widths, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added `rtl/load_mru_replay.sv`, a two-entry MRU latch for legal normal-RAM load words. Each entry tracks a valid bit, aligned word address tag, and raw 32-bit word data.
- Integrated the latch in `rtl/mem_stage.sv`.
  - Ready normal-RAM loads still use live `dmem_rdata` and fill the latch.
  - Stalled legal normal-RAM load hits suppress `dmem_ren`, clear `mem_wait`, and use cached raw data for load extraction and RVFI `mem_rdata`.
  - Replay is disabled while the pending-store slot is valid, and for stores, MMIO/marker/UART/OOB addresses, illegal accesses, and misaligned accesses.
  - Legal normal-RAM stores byte-merge matching latch entries when they retire or enqueue into the pending-store slot.

## Deviations

- No `core_pkg.sv` changes were needed; the replay eligibility stayed local to MEM.
- The implementation promotes an entry-1 replay hit to entry 0 so the two entries behave as a true MRU pair. This is a small sequential state update and does not add another table.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns from lint or formal. The latch intentionally resets only valid bits; tags and data are ignored until valid.
- This still adds two 32-bit tag compares in the MEM decision path, so the final FPGA result will determine whether the small cycle win outweighs timing cost.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before solver startup in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable worktree, and the script's staging cleanup could not remove stale files there (`Operation not permitted`).
- Equivalent local formal flow from a temporary writable mirror under `/tmp`, using the same `checks.cfg`, `wrapper.sv`, current `rtl/*.sv`, and riscv-formal framework files: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Registered Lookahead Agree Branch Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor win is the compressed registered lookahead
instruction replay table. It attacks random iStall cycles without putting a
same-cycle replay lookup on the IF decision path. Other predictor attempts
show the remaining tradeoff clearly: learned conditional-branch tables did
reduce CoreMark cycles, but their combinational IF table reads and EX update
routing cost more Fmax than the saved redirects recovered. Return predictors
and replay capacity variants had the same problem or saved too few cycles.

The most promising predictor angle is therefore to revisit dynamic branch
direction with the timing discipline that made instruction replay work.
Keep the existing static BTFNT plus direct-JAL predictor as the cold and
target-generation path, but move the learned direction-table lookup into a
registered lookahead candidate for the next fetch PC. That preserves the
current IF replay structure and avoids adding a current-cycle counter-array
read to the branch/JAL target and fetch-ready path.
hypothesis:
Add a small PC-indexed two-bit agree predictor for conditional branch
direction, but read it one cycle ahead in IF. The table is indexed by the
same selected next fetch PC used to maintain the registered instruction
replay candidate. Each cycle registers the selected counter prediction for
the PC expected to be current after the next clock edge. When IF later sees a
legal aligned B-type branch at that PC, it combines the registered
agree/disagree bit with the existing static direction rule: cold counters
reset to strongly agree, so cold behavior is exactly the current backward
taken / forward not-taken policy. Direct JAL prediction remains unchanged,
and JALR remains unpredicted.

Conditional branches update the agree table from EX when the branch advances
into EX/MEM. The update trains toward whether the resolved branch outcome
agreed with the static direction for that branch PC, and is gated so dmem
holds cannot update the same branch repeatedly. Redirect recovery remains
the existing resolved-next-PC mismatch check, so aliasing or a stale
registered prediction can only cost redirect bubbles and cannot affect RVFI
correctness.

This targets the cycle-count signal seen in the earlier agree-predictor
experiment while avoiding its biggest timing cost: the current-cycle IF
table lookup. The design adds no BTB, RAS, replay capacity change, pipeline
stage, load-data bypass, top-level IO change, or retirement-width change.
The existing registered IF replay predictor, pending-store slot, divider
interlock, strict channel-0 RVFI order sequence, and tied-off channel 1
remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add a small two-bit agree-direction table, such as 64 entries indexed by
fetch PC bits, with counters reset to strongly agree. Read the selected
counter into a registered lookahead prediction using the same next-PC
priority as the replay candidate: EX redirect target first, then the
predicted/fallthrough next PC when fetch advances, otherwise the held PC.
For legal aligned B-type branches, combine the registered agree bit with
the existing static direction rule to choose predicted_taken. Preserve
direct-JAL prediction, JALR unpredicted behavior, compressed instruction
replay tags, resetless replay payload arrays, live-imem priority,
replay fill bypass, redirect priority, and fetch_ready semantics.

Emit one-cycle branch predictor update sideband signals for valid,
non-misaligned conditional branches when EX/MEM is allowed to advance:
update_valid, update_pc, update_static_taken, and update_actual_taken.
Keep redirect generation based on resolved next PC versus the carried
predicted next PC, and do not change branch target calculation, trap
handling, divider launch/completion, forwarding, or RVFI metadata.

Wire the EX-stage conditional-branch update signals back to IF without
changing the core module IO, RVFI NRET=2 field set, channel-0-only
single retirement convention, strict rvfi_order behavior, pending-store
slot arbitration, registered instruction replay wiring, or channel-1
tie-off.

Clarify the prediction metadata comments to document that conditional
branch direction may come from a registered lookahead agree predictor,
while direct JAL and replayed instruction handling remain otherwise
unchanged. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder
legality defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Registered Lookahead Agree Branch Predictor

## What changed

- Added a 64-entry PC-indexed two-bit agree predictor in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- The agree table resets counters to strongly agree (`2'b11`), so cold conditional-branch behavior remains the existing static backward-taken / forward-not-taken rule.
- IF reads the agree counter into a registered lookahead candidate using the same selected next-PC priority as the instruction replay candidate: EX redirect target, then predicted/fallthrough next PC when fetch advances, otherwise the held PC.
- Legal aligned B-type branches now combine the registered agree/disagree bit with the static direction rule. Direct JAL prediction and JALR-unpredicted behavior are unchanged.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv` now emits one-cycle conditional-branch predictor update sideband signals when a valid non-misaligned branch is allowed to advance into EX/MEM.
- `rtl/core.sv` wires the EX update sideband back to IF without changing the top-level IO.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comments now document that conditional branch direction may come from the registered lookahead agree predictor.

## Deviations

- The agree table uses 64 entries as suggested. It is direct-mapped and untagged; aliasing is intentionally handled by normal EX redirect recovery.
- Simultaneous update and lookahead read of the same agree-table entry uses the pre-update counter value for the registered candidate. This keeps the IF path simple and still only affects prediction quality.
- A Verilator `UNUSEDSIGNAL` pragma marks the unused high and low bits of `bp_update_pc` in IF, because the table intentionally indexes only `pc[7:2]`.

## Concerns

- Aliasing or a stale registered agree bit can train/predict the wrong direction, but any bad prediction is corrected by the existing resolved-next-PC mismatch redirect.
- The in-tree `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected path outside the writable worktree, causing `rm -f "$CORE_DIR"/*.sv` to fail with `Operation not permitted` before any checks ran.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before checks by the sandbox staging issue described above.
- Equivalent formal run from a throwaway `/tmp` mirror of `formal/` and `rtl/`, using the same `run_all.sh`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Source-Qualified Load-Use Interlock
motivation:
The current best RTL has already harvested the high-leverage Fmax wins:
DIV/REM is cold and iterative, the IF replay predictor hides most random
iStall loss, replay tags are compressed/resetless, the RVFI order counter is
segmented, and the ALU result mux has a hot/cold split. The next micro-opt
should therefore avoid new memories, predictors, queues, or arithmetic units.

The remaining always-present cycle loss in the control path is the
conservative load-use detector. It currently stalls whenever the load in
ID/EX has a nonzero rd matching either raw rs1 or raw rs2 bitfield of the
IF/ID instruction. That is necessary for true consumers, but false for many
legal RV32I encodings: OP-IMM, LOAD, and JALR do not read rs2; LUI, AUIPC,
JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK do not read either source. CoreMark has many pointer
updates, immediate arithmetic, loads, and control instructions after loads,
so these false matches can still inject bubbles even after the instruction
replay predictor improves frontend availability.
hypothesis:
Refine the load-use interlock so it only compares the ID/EX load destination
against source operands that the IF/ID instruction actually reads. Add a
small source-use predecode in the hazard path with conservative defaults:
rs1 is used by OP, OP-IMM, LOAD, STORE, BRANCH, and JALR; rs2 is used by OP,
STORE, and BRANCH; LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and the exact EBREAK encoding are
source-free. Unknown opcodes and reserved/unclear SYSTEM encodings remain
conservative and are treated as using both raw source fields so illegal-trap
RVFI behavior is not weakened.

Gate the predicate with both stage valid bits: a bubble in ID/EX is not a
load producer, and an invalid IF/ID payload has no consumer sources. True
load-use dependencies keep the exact existing one-cycle bubble, including
load-to-store address dependencies and load-to-branch comparisons. The change
does not add a MEM-to-EX data path, alter forwarding, change memory ordering,
touch the IF replay predictor, change the divider interlock, or modify any
RVFI field or top-level IO. The expected benefit is a small CoreMark cycle
reduction with limited Fmax risk because the added logic is only a compact
opcode/source-use filter in the existing hazard predicate.

changes.description:
Replace the raw rs1-or-rs2 load-use comparison with a source-qualified
predicate. Add inputs for IF/ID valid, ID/EX valid, and the IF/ID
instruction or opcode/funct fields. Compute uses_rs1 and uses_rs2 from
the opcode with conservative handling for unknown or unclear encodings,
and stall only when the load rd matches a source that is actually read.
Preserve the existing imem replay readiness, redirect flush, dmem wait,
EX long-operation, EX/MEM hold, and MEM/WB hold behavior.

Wire IF/ID valid, ID/EX valid, and the IF/ID instruction or needed
opcode/funct fields into the hazard unit. Keep the core module IO, RVFI
NRET=2 port set, channel-0-only retirement behavior, strict rvfi_order
sequence, registered IF replay wiring, pending-store-slot arbitration,
and tied-off channel 1 unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Source-Qualified Load-Use Interlock

## What changed

- Added `rtl/source_use_decode.sv`, a compact shared predecode for architectural source usage.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` so load-use stalls require:
  - a valid ID/EX load producer,
  - a valid IF/ID consumer,
  - a nonzero matching load destination,
  - and a match only against source operands the IF/ID instruction actually reads.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` to wire IF/ID and ID/EX valid bits plus the IF/ID instruction into the hazard unit.
- Added a MEM-stage RVFI source-data repair path from the current WB value. This keeps the core's existing raw RVFI source-address convention intact for cosim while ensuring a false raw source match behind a load still reports the current architectural source data.
- Qualified EX/MEM forwarding so loads are not forwarded from EX/MEM. True load consumers still wait for the existing load-use bubble and receive data from MEM/WB; this prevents false consumers from recording a load address as raw RVFI source data.

## Deviations

- I added a small `source_use_decode` module instead of open-coding the opcode mask directly inside `hazard_unit`.
- I kept raw RVFI source addresses because the existing cosim reference compares them directly for every instruction. Instead of masking unused sources to x0/0, `mem_stage` repairs raw source data from the immediately older WB instruction when needed. This affects RVFI metadata only; it does not feed EX operands or add a load-data bypass for execution.
- EX/MEM forwarding for loads is now explicitly disabled. The old forwarding select was harmless only because the conservative load-use interlock kept true consumers away from that path; after source qualification, false consumers can reach EX while a load is in MEM, so forwarding the load address would corrupt raw RVFI source metadata.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns after lint, formal, and cosim.
- The added source-use decode is combinational in the existing hazard path. The MEM-stage RVFI repair adds small compare/mux logic on source metadata; FPGA timing will determine the actual Fmax impact.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before solver startup because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected checkout outside the writable sandbox, and the script's staging cleanup failed with `Operation not permitted`.
- Equivalent formal run from `/tmp/codex-formal-hyp-20260428-046-r18s0`, using the same `run_all.sh`, `checks.cfg`, `wrapper.sv`, current `rtl/*.sv`, and riscv-formal framework files: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.
- `make cosim`: pass, `elfs_tested: 2`.
- Intermediate formal attempts failed `reg_ch0` on false raw source dependencies. The first counterexample showed stale raw source data behind a load; the second showed an AUIPC recording a misaligned load's address through EX/MEM forwarding. The WB-to-MEM metadata repair plus load-qualified EX/MEM forwarding fixed those failures while preserving raw RVFI source addresses.

title: Decode-Exact Load-Use Interlock
motivation:
The current best core has strong timing wins from the registered instruction
replay predictor, segmented RVFI order counter, pending-store slot, and
hot/cold ALU split. The remaining structural improvement should therefore
avoid another cache, queue, predictor table, or load-data forwarding path that
could disturb Fmax.

The load-use interlock is still structurally conservative. It stalls whenever
a load in ID/EX has a nonzero destination matching either raw rs1 or raw rs2
bitfield of the IF/ID instruction. That is correct for true consumers, but it
creates false bubbles for instructions where one or both fields are not
source operands: OP-IMM and LOAD do not read rs2, JAL/LUI/AUIPC read neither,
FENCE/EBREAK read neither, and JALR reads only rs1. CoreMark's optimized code
has dense immediate arithmetic, address-generation loads, calls, and loop
control, so these false one-cycle bubbles are a plausible remaining IPC loss.

A prior operand-aware interlock attempt broke selftest, so this hypothesis is
deliberately conservative: decode source-use exactly for known legal
instruction classes, keep reserved or unknown encodings on the current
both-source stall policy, and do not add any MEM-to-EX load-data path. The
goal is to recover only provably false load-use stalls while leaving all true
load consumers, dmem waits, branch recovery, divider interlocks, and RVFI
ordering unchanged.
hypothesis:
Add a small decode-light source-use classifier for the IF/ID instruction and
use it in the load-use hazard predicate. A load in ID/EX should stall the next
instruction only when the next instruction is valid and actually reads the
matching source:

- rs1 is used by OP, OP-IMM, LOAD, STORE, BRANCH, and legal JALR.
- rs2 is used by OP, STORE, and BRANCH.
- JAL, LUI, AUIPC, FENCE/FENCE.I, and EBREAK use no integer source register.
- Reserved funct3/funct7 combinations and unknown opcodes remain
  conservative by treating both rs fields as possible sources.

The existing one-cycle bubble remains for every true load-use dependency,
including store address dependencies, branch compares, JALR bases, ALU
consumers, and any conservative illegal/unknown instruction class. The change
should not forward load data, alter the MEM stage, widen retirement, or
change the top-level IO. It only refines when the frontend and ID/EX register
are stalled for a load in EX.

This preserves the hard invariants: decoder legality still defaults illegal,
RVFI traps and order remain generated at MEM/WB, channel 1 stays tied off, and
all memory and timing-marker behavior remains unchanged. The expected benefit
is a small CoreMark cycle reduction with minimal Fmax risk because the added
logic is opcode/funct predecode on the existing hazard path, not a new data
path or stateful predictor.

changes.description:
Replace the raw load-use comparison against both IF/ID register fields
with a source-use-aware predicate. Add conservative decode-light logic for
known legal RV32IM instruction classes, defaulting unknown or reserved
encodings to uses_rs1=1 and uses_rs2=1. Gate the rs1 comparison with
uses_rs1 and the rs2 comparison with uses_rs2, while preserving the
existing redirect, imem replay readiness, MEM wait, and EX long-operation
stall behavior.

Pass any additional IF/ID instruction validity or predecoded source-use
sideband needed by the hazard unit. Keep the core module IO unchanged,
preserve the registered IF replay wiring, pending-store-slot arbitration,
divider interlock, RVFI NRET=2 field set, strict channel-0 order sequence,
and tied-off channel 1.

If useful, add compact comments or bundle fields documenting IF/ID
source-use metadata for hazard detection. Do not change RVFI fields,
architectural state semantics, decoder legality defaults, ALU op
encodings, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Kill-Gated I-Fetch Replay Fills
motivation:
The current best design already has the predictor feature with the strongest
measured payoff: a 256-entry registered lookahead instruction replay table
with compressed tags. It hides random iStall bubbles without putting a
same-cycle table lookup on the IF decision path. Follow-up predictor attempts
that added branch-direction state, return targets, victim entries, wider
replay lines, or replay-cached next-PC metadata either saved too few cycles
or lost more Fmax than they recovered.

The next predictor slot should therefore keep the existing replay structure
and target replay quality rather than add another table. The current IF
stage refreshes the replay table on every live `io_imemReady` fetch, even
when that fetch is being killed by an EX redirect or IF flush. Those killed
words are architecturally correct immutable instructions, but they are
wrong-path for the current execution and can evict useful hot-loop replay
entries in the direct-mapped table. Avoiding this pollution is a narrow
replay-predictor improvement with little timing exposure: it changes the
replay write-enable policy, not the current-cycle fetch-ready, branch/JAL
target, ALU, memory, or retirement paths.
hypothesis:
Gate instruction replay-table fills so only instructions that are eligible to
enter the frontend stream refresh the replay predictor. A live imem word
should update the replay entry when `io_imemReady` is high and the IF payload
is not being killed by reset, flush, or EX redirect. Backend stalls that hold
the current PC, such as load-use, divide-long, or MEM wait, should still be
allowed to refresh the held-PC replay entry because that instruction remains
the next architectural fetch once the stall clears.

On redirect or flush cycles, suppress both the direct replay-table write and
any fill-bypass path that would seed the registered lookahead candidate from
the killed live word. The next candidate should instead come from the
existing table lookup for the selected redirect target or held PC. Live imem
data still has priority whenever it is not killed, replay hits still use the
registered candidate during external imem stalls, replay misses still hold
the PC and inject the existing IF bubble, and the static backward-branch plus
direct-JAL predictor policy remains unchanged.

This preserves correctness because replay entries are only a performance
predictor for immutable instruction words; suppressing a killed fill can only
turn a future replay hit into a miss. RVFI instruction reporting,
resolved-next-PC recovery, strict channel-0 `rvfi_order`, tied-off channel 1,
pending-store-slot behavior, divider interlock, decoder legality rules, and
top-level IO remain unchanged. The expected benefit is a modest CoreMark
cycle reduction from less direct-mapped replay pollution, with minimal Fmax
risk because the added gating is on replay-table write/update enables rather
than the hot current-cycle prediction datapath.

changes.description:
Add an explicit `replay_fill_enable` derived from live imem readiness
and the existing IF kill conditions. Use it for replay-table writes and
for the same-index fill-bypass candidate path so reset, flush, and EX
redirect cycles cannot seed replay state from a killed wrong-path word.
Keep fill enabled during ordinary backend stalls that hold the current
PC. Preserve the 256-entry compressed-tag replay table, resetless payload
arrays, registered lookahead candidate with current-PC guard, live-imem
priority, replay-hit `fetch_ready` semantics, and the existing static
backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction policy.

Clarify only the IF/ID replay comments to document that replay entries
are refreshed from live imem words that are not killed by reset, flush,
or redirect, while held-PC backend stalls may still refresh the replay
entry. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality
defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Added `replay_fill_enable` in `rtl/if_stage.sv`, derived as `imem_ready && !fetch_kill`.
- Used `replay_fill_enable` for replay-table writes, so live imem words killed by reset, IF flush, or EX redirect no longer refresh replay state.
- Used `replay_fill_enable` for the same-index fill-bypass path, so killed live words cannot seed the registered lookahead replay candidate.
- Updated the IF/ID replay comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document killed-fill suppression and held-PC backend-stall refresh behavior.

## Deviations

- No structural changes were needed. The existing replay lookup target selection already chooses the redirect target on redirect and the held PC during stalls.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. Backend stalls that hold the PC still allow live imem fills because they do not assert the IF kill conditions.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the worktree was blocked before checks by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable roots and the script stages files under that tree.
- Re-ran the same `run_all.sh` from a temporary writable `/tmp/auto_arch_formal_r18s2` staging tree with this worktree's `rtl/` linked in: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bitfield RV32M Control Decode
motivation:
The current best result came from making the ALU opcode encoding expose a
hot RV32I path and a cold RV32M path. That change improved Fmax without
changing CoreMark cycles, which suggests the next micro-opt should keep
exploiting the same encoding rather than adding another replay table,
predictor, queue, reset rewrite, or arithmetic unit.

Some downstream control logic still decodes RV32M operations with explicit
equality trees. In particular, EX detects divide/remainder instructions with
four full `alu_op == ALU_*` compares even though the current encoding makes
DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU exactly the `alu_op[4] && alu_op[2]` class. The divider
also reclassifies signed versus unsigned and quotient versus remainder with
opcode equality checks even though those are now just low funct3 bits inside
the divide group.

These compares are small, but they sit in always-present EX control and
long-operation stall logic that feeds the frontend hold network. Recent
experiments show broad datapath, reset, and replay changes often lose more
placement quality than they recover, so a narrow decode cleanup in the
already-proven ALU-op encoding is the most attractive micro_opt follow-up.
hypothesis:
Replace redundant RV32M opcode equality decoders with bitfield tests derived
from the current hot/cold ALU encoding. In EX, compute divide-class detection
as `alu_op[4] && alu_op[2]` instead of OR-ing four equality comparisons for
DIV, DIVU, REM, and REMU. This remains exact because MUL/MULH/MULHSU/MULHU
are encoded as `5'b10000` through `5'b10011`, while DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU are
encoded as `5'b10100` through `5'b10111`.

In the real divider path, derive `want_rem` from `op[1]` and signedness from
`!op[0]` within the divide-class group, while keeping the existing ALTOPS
case labels for formal substitutions. The divider still launches only from
EX's divide-class predicate and still preserves all RV32M corner cases:
divide-by-zero, signed INT_MIN/-1 overflow, REM-by-zero, and signed
remainder sign correction.

This does not alter any arithmetic result, instruction latency, forwarding
path, branch predictor, memory behavior, pending-store slot, replay table,
top-level IO, RVFI field, channel-0 retirement order, or channel-1 tie-off.
CoreMark cycle count should be unchanged. The expected gain is a small median
Fmax improvement from replacing several full opcode comparators in EX and
divider control with direct low-bit tests that match the current ALU encoding.

changes.description:
Replace the four-way DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU equality OR used for
`is_div_op` with the exact bitfield predicate
`in.ctrl.alu_op[4] && in.ctrl.alu_op[2]`. Preserve the existing
`in.valid`, illegal-instruction, stall, divider launch/completion,
EX/MEM bubble, forwarding, redirect, and RVFI metadata behavior.

In the real RV32M divider implementation, derive divide/remainder
classification from the encoded funct3 low bits: `op[1]` selects
REM/REMU versus DIV/DIVU, and `!op[0]` selects signed DIV/REM versus
unsigned DIVU/REMU. Keep all existing special-case arithmetic semantics
and keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS formulas keyed by the named ALU
constants so formal behavior remains unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: replaced the four named DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU equality compares for `is_div_op` with the encoded divide-class predicate `in.ctrl.alu_op[4] && in.ctrl.alu_op[2]`.
- `rtl/div_unit.sv`: in the real divider path, replaced named-op equality classification with `op[1]` for quotient-vs-remainder and `!op[0]` for signed-vs-unsigned.
- Kept the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` case labels keyed by the named `ALU_*` constants.

## Deviations
- Added a local Verilator `UNUSEDSIGNAL` guard on the divider `op` input. In the real divider build, `op[4:2]` are now intentionally consumed by EX-stage launch decode, while the divider result control only needs `op[1:0]`. This keeps the required lint command warning-clean without adding decode logic back into the real divider path.

## Concerns
- None known. The change assumes the existing decoder continues to be the only source of launched divider ops, matching the current `ALU_*` encoding.

## Verification
- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: the direct in-place run was blocked by sandbox permissions while deleting staged files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`.
- Equivalent local formal run from a `/tmp` mirror excluding the protected `riscv-formal/cores` staging tree: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Backend-Stall Shadow I-Fetch Prefetcher
motivation:
The current best core gets its largest cycle-count win from the registered
lookahead instruction replay table: hot instructions can be supplied during
random imem stalls without a same-cycle replay lookup on the IF critical
path. The remaining structural stalls are now mostly backend stalls from
dmem waits and long operations. During those cycles the frontend PC is held,
decode is not accepting a new instruction, and the independent imem port is
usually wasted refreshing the same held PC or sitting idle.

Prior fetch queues tried to decouple fetch and decode directly, but they put
queue control on the normal zero-wait frontend path and lost Fmax. A more
targeted structural angle is to use backend-stall cycles only as replay-table
warmup time. The architectural fetch/decode stream remains held exactly as it
is today, while a shadow prefetch PC walks ahead through the existing static
branch/direct-JAL prediction policy and fills the replay table with future
instruction words. When the backend releases, later external imem stalls are
more likely to hit in the already-proven registered replay structure.
hypothesis:
Add a shadow instruction prefetch mode inside IF that activates only while a
backend stall is holding the architectural frontend and the current IF/ID
instruction is already safely held or replayable. On entry, capture the
current architectural IF/ID metadata into a tiny hold register so the hazard
unit and decode-facing output continue to see the same instruction while
the external imem address is repurposed for prefetch. The architectural PC
and pipeline-visible IF/ID payload remain frozen until the backend accepts
them; only the replay table is updated by shadow fetches.

While shadow prefetch is active, drive `io_imemAddr` from a `prefetch_pc`
rather than the architectural PC. On each `io_imemReady` prefetch response,
fill or refresh the existing 256-entry compressed-tag replay entry for
`prefetch_pc`, then advance `prefetch_pc` using the same zero-state static
next-PC rule already used for normal fetch: backward conditional branches
predict taken, direct aligned JAL predicts its target, and JALR remains
unpredicted. Redirects, reset, and frontend flushes cancel shadow prefetch
immediately and resume normal fetch at the resolved architectural target.

This is not a fetch queue and it does not let prefetched instructions enter
decode early. It only converts cycles where the backend cannot accept an
instruction into useful instruction replay-table fills. Live architectural
fetches still have priority when the backend is ready, replay hits still use
the registered lookahead candidate, replay misses still hold the PC and
inject the existing IF bubble, and EX still repairs branch/JAL prediction
mistakes through resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery. The top-level IO, RVFI
NRET=2 port set, channel-0-only retirement convention, strict `rvfi_order`
sequence, pending-store slot, divider interlock, memory ordering, and decoder
legality rules remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add a backend-stall-only shadow prefetch mode. Capture the current
architectural IF/ID metadata into a hold register before repurposing
`io_imemAddr`, keep the decode-facing output stable from that hold
register while the backend is stalled, and use a separate `prefetch_pc`
to fill the existing compressed-tag replay table. Advance the shadow PC
with the existing static branch/direct-JAL prediction rule, cancel it on
reset, flush, or EX redirect, and preserve live-imem priority plus the
registered replay-hit `fetch_ready` contract for architectural fetches.

Wire a backend-prefetch-eligible signal into IF, derived from precise
backend holds such as MEM wait and EX long-operation stalls rather than
raw imem misses. Keep the core module IO, RVFI channel layout, pending
store-slot arbitration, hot/cold ALU wiring, divider interlock, and
channel-1 tie-off unchanged.

If needed, expose or document the distinction between frontend imem
unavailability and backend holds so IF only enters shadow prefetch when
decode is blocked by the backend and the architectural IF/ID instruction
must remain stable. Preserve existing load-use, redirect, MEM wait,
EX-long-operation, EX/MEM hold, and MEM/WB hold behavior.

Clarify IF/ID comments to document that during backend-stall shadow
prefetch the pipeline-visible IF/ID metadata remains held while the imem
port may be used only to warm the replay table. Do not change bundle
widths, architectural state semantics, decoder legality defaults, RVFI
fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: XOR-Indexed I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor state is the compressed, resetless registered
lookahead instruction replay table. It preserves the large random-iStall
cycle reduction of the 256-entry replay predictor while keeping the
current-cycle IF path free of a combinational table read. Follow-up
predictor attempts show the boundary clearly: extra branch-direction state,
return targets, replay victim entries, replay-line widening, and cached
next-PC metadata either saved too few cycles or lost more Fmax than they
recovered.

The remaining replay opportunity is likely direct-mapped conflict behavior.
The table is indexed by `pc[9:2]`, so hot instructions separated by 1 KiB
alias even though the CoreMark timed region repeatedly visits several loop
bodies and helper routines. Smaller tables lost hit rate, and adding ways or
victim entries added too much logic. A hashed direct-map index targets the
same conflict misses while keeping the table size, registered lookahead
timing model, live-imem-first policy, and compressed tag storage intact.
hypothesis:
Replace the replay table's direct index `pc[9:2]` with a simple XOR-folded
index, for example `pc[9:2] ^ pc[17:10]`, for both live fills and registered
lookahead lookups. Keep the existing 256-entry table, high-PC tag compare,
resetless tag/instruction payload arrays, registered candidate PC guard,
same-entry fill bypass, and live `io_imemData` priority.

For a fixed stored high tag `pc[31:10]`, the XOR fold is still one-to-one
with the low word index, so the existing compressed tag plus registered
current-PC guard remains correctness-preserving. A hash collision can only
evict a useful replay word and create a future miss; it cannot inject an
instruction for the wrong PC. Replay misses still hold the PC and inject the
existing IF bubble, while replay hits continue to feed the cached instruction
into the current static backward-branch and direct-JAL prediction path.

This is intended to recover iStall cycles lost to 1 KiB direct-map conflicts
without adding another table, way, victim search, branch update path, RAS,
pipeline stage, or load-data bypass. The top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 channel
layout, strict channel-0 order sequence, tied-off channel 1, pending-store
slot, divider interlock, decoder legality rules, and branch/JAL resolved
next-PC recovery remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Change the registered lookahead replay predictor index from the raw
direct-mapped `pc[9:2]` bits to a small XOR-folded index such as

same-entry fill-bypass detection. Preserve the 256-entry table size,
compressed `pc[31:10]` tags, resetless replay payload arrays, candidate
PC equality guard, live-imem priority, replay-hit `fetch_ready`
semantics, redirect priority, and the existing static backward-branch
plus direct-JAL prediction behavior.

Update only the IF/ID replay comments to document that replay entries
use an XOR-folded direct-map index plus the existing high-PC tag and
registered current-PC guard. Do not change bundle widths, architectural
state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the
NRET=2 contract.

title: Funct3-Aligned Hot ALU Encoding
motivation:
The current best result came from an RTL-only hot/cold ALU opcode split:
RV32M operations were moved behind `alu_op[4]`, letting the common RV32I
result path avoid the cold M-extension mux. That improved Fmax without
changing CoreMark cycles, so the most promising micro_opt follow-up is to
keep refining the same hot EX datapath rather than adding new replay state,
queues, reset rewrites, or arithmetic units.

The remaining RV32I/simple ALU encoding is still arbitrary and dense:
ADD/SUB/logic/compare/shift/LUI are assigned sequential localparams, so the
decoder emits constants unrelated to the instruction funct3 bits and the ALU
still needs an 11-way simple-result case. RISC-V already groups the hot
OP/OP-IMM operations by funct3, with only SUB/SRA needing a modifier bit.
Re-encoding the simple ALU controls around that structure should shrink
decode and mux logic on the single-cycle EX result path while preserving the
successful `op[4]` M-extension discriminator.
hypothesis:
Re-encode the RV32I/simple ALU operations so `alu_op[2:0]` matches the
RISC-V funct3 value for ADD/SUB, SLL, SLT, SLTU, XOR, SRL/SRA, OR, and AND,
while `alu_op[3]` distinguishes the alternate ADD/SUB and SRL/SRA variants.
Keep `alu_op[4]=0` for every simple operation and keep all RV32M constants
at the current `alu_op[4]=1` contiguous encoding that produced the best
result. Assign LUI to one otherwise-unused simple encoding, such as
`op[3]=1` with low bits `111`, and keep AUIPC as ADD with operand A = PC as
today.

Refactor the hot simple ALU mux to decode mostly by `op[2:0]`: one case arm
each for ADD/SUB, SLL, SLT, SLTU, XOR, SRL/SRA, OR, and AND/LUI, with
`op[3]` only used inside the ADD/SUB, SRL/SRA, and LUI-vs-AND arms. The cold
M mux and final `op[4] ? m_out : simple_out` selection remain unchanged.

Decoder behavior stays architecturally identical: legal OP and OP-IMM
instructions emit the new funct3-aligned constants, reserved encodings still
leave `is_illegal=1`, and every RVFI-visible arithmetic result is unchanged.
No instruction latency, divider protocol, forwarding path, branch predictor,
memory behavior, replay table, top-level IO, RVFI field, channel-0
retirement ordering, or channel-1 tie-off changes. CoreMark cycles should be
identical; the expected gain is a modest median Fmax improvement from a
shallower hot ALU control/mux path.

changes.description:
Reassign the RV32I/simple ALU localparams so the low three bits match
instruction funct3 where possible, with bit 3 as the SUB/SRA/simple
special modifier. Preserve the current `alu_op[4]=1` RV32M constants
and do not change any bundle widths, RVFI fields, architectural state,
decoder legality defaults, or the NRET=2 contract.

Emit the new funct3-aligned ALU constants for OP and OP-IMM decode.
ADD/ADDI and the logical/compare/shift operations should map directly
from funct3, while SUB and SRA/SRAI set the modifier bit. LUI keeps its
dedicated simple encoding, AUIPC remains the ADD path with `is_auipc`,
and all reserved opcode/funct combinations must retain the existing
illegal-default behavior.

Rewrite the `simple_out` mux around the new encoding: case primarily on
`op[2:0]`, use `op[3]` only for ADD-vs-SUB, SRL-vs-SRA, and
AND-vs-LUI, and keep the cold M-extension mux plus ALTOPS multiply
substitutions unchanged. The final output remains selected by
`op[4] ? m_out : simple_out`.

title: Same-Word Store Slot Coalescing
motivation:
The current best core already includes the structural wins that have survived
FPGA evaluation: a one-deep pending-store slot for normal-RAM store stalls
and a registered instruction replay table for random iStall. Broader store
buffering attempts found the right bottleneck but were too expensive:
two-entry pending-store designs either failed placement, and the same-cycle
refill variant also failed placement. That suggests the next structural
store-side step should add capacity only for cases the existing single slot
can handle locally.

CoreMark updates list nodes, matrix cells, stack frames, and scalar state with
short bursts of stores. When a pending normal-RAM store is already occupying
the slot and dmem is not ready, a younger store to the same aligned word does
not need a second physical buffer entry. It can be merged into the pending
slot because loads and MMIO are still held behind the slot, and there is no
architectural observer between the two normal-RAM stores. This targets the
same store-burst pressure as the failed two-entry FIFO, but with one address
compare and one byte-mask merge instead of another queue entry or a wider
dmem arbiter.
hypothesis:
Add same-word coalescing to the existing one-entry pending-store slot. When
the slot is valid, io_dmemReady is low, and the MEM-stage instruction is a
legal aligned normal-RAM store whose word-aligned address matches the pending
slot's word address, retire the younger store through MEM/WB and merge its
byte lanes into the slot entry. For overlapping byte lanes, the younger store
wins; for disjoint lanes, keep the older bytes and add the younger mask bits.
The physical dmem port remains idle until the slot later drains, at which
point it writes the merged word/mask once.

Preserve the current direct fast path when the slot is empty and dmem is
ready, and preserve the current low-ready enqueue path when the slot is empty.
Do not merge stores while the older slot is draining on a ready cycle; that
avoids the placement-heavy same-cycle refill case that already failed. Loads,
MMIO/marker/UART stores, non-normal-RAM stores, misaligned stores, illegal or
trapping accesses, and stores to a different word still wait behind the valid
slot exactly as today. RVFI still retires every store once with that
instruction's own mem_addr, mem_wmask, and mem_wdata; the later physical drain
of a coalesced slot produces no additional RVFI event.

This is a structural store-stall reduction, not a load-data bypass or a wider
retirement design. It keeps the top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 port set,
channel-0-only retirement convention, strict rvfi_order sequence, registered
IF replay predictor, divider interlock, branch prediction, decoder legality,
and timing-marker behavior unchanged. The expected benefit is a small cycle
reduction on same-word store bursts under random dStall with much lower
placement risk than a second store-buffer entry.

changes.description:
Extend the one-entry pending-store slot with a same-word merge input.
When merge is asserted while the slot is valid and not draining, update
the stored replicated write data and byte mask so younger byte lanes
override older lanes and disjoint lanes are ORed into the pending mask.
Keep the existing enqueue, valid, and drain behavior for all non-merge
cases; the slot still stores only physical drain fields, not RVFI
bookkeeping.

Detect merge-eligible stores: valid legal aligned normal-RAM stores whose
word-aligned address matches a valid pending store slot while dmem is not
ready. Allow those stores to retire without asserting mem_wait, emit their
normal RVFI memory fields, and request a slot merge instead of a new live
dmem write. Keep loads, nonbufferable stores, stores to other words,
traps, and pending-slot ready-drain cases on the existing wait path.

Wire the store-slot merge request/data/mask signals between MEM and the
pending-store slot, and expose the slot's word address to MEM for the
same-word comparison. Preserve the current dmem arbitration priority:
a valid pending slot drives the external port, and live MEM-stage
requests use the direct path only when the slot is empty.

Continue consuming the MEM-stage mem_wait interlock. Update comments if
needed to document that a same-word normal-RAM store may retire by
coalescing into a non-draining pending slot, while all other memory
operations behind a valid slot remain blocked. Do not change imem replay
readiness, load-use behavior, EX long-operation stalls, redirect flushes,
EX/MEM holds, or MEM/WB hold semantics.

# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Extended `rtl/store_slot.sv` with a merge path for a younger store to the same aligned word while the slot is valid and not draining.
- The slot still holds only physical drain fields: byte address, replicated write data, and byte mask.
- Merge updates the stored byte lanes with `(old & ~younger_mask) | (younger & younger_mask)` and ORs the byte masks, so overlapping lanes take the younger store data.
- Exposed the slot's word-aligned address to `rtl/mem_stage.sv` for the same-word comparison.
- Updated `rtl/mem_stage.sv` to assert `store_slot_merge` for valid, legal, aligned normal-RAM stores when:
  - the pending slot is valid,
  - `io_dmemReady` is low,

- A coalesced store retires through MEM/WB with its own RVFI `mem_addr`, `mem_wmask`, and `mem_wdata`; the later physical drain creates no RVFI event.
- Loads, non-normal-RAM stores, MMIO/marker/UART stores, misaligned memory ops, and stores to other words continue to wait behind a valid slot.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` wiring and the `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` comment for the refined `mem_wait` behavior.

title: Banked Registered I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor structure is the 256-entry registered lookahead
instruction replay table with compressed resetless tags. It is the only
predictor-family change with a large measured cycle signal, because it turns
random imem stalls into useful hot-instruction replays while keeping the
current-cycle IF path free of a combinational table read. Follow-up
predictor attempts that added branch-direction state, return targets,
victim entries, wider lines, cached next-PC metadata, or XOR indexing either
saved too few cycles or lost more Fmax than they recovered.

The best remaining replay opportunity is to reduce direct-mapped conflict
misses without adding an associative lookup or changing the current-cycle
fetch-ready path. A full victim buffer added several tag compares and
regressed. XOR indexing changed conflicts but did not improve the measured
hit-rate/Fmax product. A banked direct-mapped predictor is a narrower
alternative: keep two small direct-mapped replay banks and choose between
them with a simple per-set MRU bit during registered lookahead generation.
The current cycle still consumes only the already registered replay
candidate, so any extra bank selection work stays off the IF replay-hit path.
hypothesis:
Replace the single 256-entry direct-mapped instruction replay table in
`if_stage` with a two-bank banked replay predictor. Each bank has 128 direct
mapped entries indexed by `pc[8:2]`, with a compressed high-PC tag
`pc[31:9]`, a valid bit, and a 32-bit instruction word. A one-bit per-set
MRU selector records which bank most recently held the line for that index.

On live `io_imemReady=1` fetches, keep the current live-imem-first behavior:
use `io_imemData` for the pipeline and refresh one bank entry for the current
PC. If either bank already matches the current tag, update that bank and set
the MRU bit to it. If neither bank matches, fill the non-MRU bank and then
mark that bank MRU. This gives each low-index set two recent tags without a
fully-associative victim search.

During registered lookahead generation for the selected next fetch PC, read
both banks for that next PC's index, compare their compressed tags, and
register the direct hit candidate with bank 0 preferred only if both banks
somehow match. The current IF cycle still selects only between live
`io_imemData` and the previously registered candidate guarded by the full
candidate PC match, so `fetch_ready` timing remains essentially the current
best design. Same-cycle fill bypass should cover either bank when the
lookahead PC is the word just filled.

Reset clears only valid bits, MRU bits, and the registered candidate valid
state; tag and instruction payload arrays remain resetless behind valid
bits. Redirects and reset flush only the in-flight IF payload and recompute
the next candidate from the selected architectural PC. Replay misses keep
the existing PC-hold and IF-bubble behavior, and EX continues to repair
branch/JAL prediction mistakes through the resolved-next-PC mismatch path.
Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, strict
`rvfi_order`, the pending-store slot, divider interlock, hot/cold ALU
encoding, decoder legality rules, and static backward-branch/direct-JAL
prediction policy remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Reorganize the registered lookahead instruction replay predictor into
two 128-entry direct-mapped banks with compressed high-PC tags and
resetless instruction payloads. Add one MRU bit per index to choose the
replacement bank on live fills. During lookahead generation, read both
banks for the selected next PC and register a candidate on a matching
bank hit; keep the current-cycle replay selection live-imem-first and
dependent only on the previously registered candidate. Preserve redirect
priority, fill bypass, fetch_ready semantics, and the existing static
backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction behavior.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to describe the two-bank registered
lookahead replay predictor and its MRU replacement policy. Do not change
bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI
fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Replaced the single 256-entry IF replay table in `rtl/if_stage.sv` with two 128-entry direct-mapped banks.
- Each bank is indexed by `pc[8:2]`, tagged with `pc[31:9]`, and stores one 32-bit instruction word behind a per-bank valid bit.
- Added one MRU bit per index. Live `imem_ready` fills update a matching bank when possible; otherwise they replace the non-MRU bank and mark that bank MRU.
- Registered lookahead generation now checks both banks for the selected next fetch PC, with bank 0 preferred if both banks match.
- Kept the current IF-cycle replay path live-imem-first and gated only by the previously registered candidate plus full candidate-PC equality.
- Preserved same-cycle fill bypass for the just-filled word, redirect priority over stalls, PC-hold replay-miss behavior, and existing static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction.
- Updated the IF/ID replay predictor comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`.

## Deviations

- No architectural or interface deviations from the hypothesis.
- The first fill after reset goes to bank 1 because MRU bits reset to 0 and replacement uses the non-MRU bank. This follows the specified MRU policy; the reset MRU value is arbitrary because both bank valid bits are clear.

## Concerns

- The two-bank lookahead path adds one extra tag compare and a small instruction mux during candidate registration. It remains off the current-cycle fetch-ready/replay-hit path.
- The original `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not run in-place in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable roots, and the script restages files there. I ran the same script from a writable `/tmp` mirror instead.

title: Bank-Aware Replay Fill Select
motivation:
The current best core is dominated by the banked registered instruction
replay predictor plus the hot/cold ALU split. The replay predictor is now
both the largest remaining IPC win and one of the most timing-sensitive IF
structures. Follow-up replay experiments show that changing capacity,
indexing, victim search, or cached next-PC metadata usually loses more Fmax
than it recovers, so the next micro-opt should keep the successful two-bank
organization and trim redundant logic inside it.

The current banked replay table still does two same-cycle tag comparisons for
every live imem fill:
`replay_fill_bank0_match` and `replay_fill_bank1_match` re-read the current
PC's indexed set to decide whether to refresh bank 0, refresh bank 1, or
replace the non-MRU bank. But the registered lookahead candidate for the
current fetch PC was produced by comparing exactly those two banks on the
previous cycle. If that candidate hits and its PC guard matches the current
PC, it already identifies that the word is present and which bank supplied
it. If it misses, the previous lookup already proved that neither bank held
the current tag, so replacement can use the non-MRU bank directly.

Reusing the registered candidate's bank for live fills should remove two
current-PC tag compare trees and part of the fill-bank muxing from the IF
replay block while keeping the current replay hit rate, table size, MRU
policy, candidate PC guard, and live-imem-first behavior intact.
hypothesis:
Extend the registered replay candidate with one bank bit indicating whether
the registered instruction came from bank 0 or bank 1. During normal
registered lookahead generation, set this bit from the bank hit selected for
`replay_lookup_pc`; during same-index fill bypass, set it to the bank chosen
for the live fill.

On a live `io_imemReady=1` fetch, choose the replay fill bank without
recomparing the current PC against both bank tags. If
`replay_cand_valid_q && replay_cand_pc_q == pc`, refresh the bank recorded in
the registered candidate and update the MRU bit to that bank. Otherwise,
treat the current PC as a replay miss and fill the non-MRU bank for that
index, then mark that bank MRU. This preserves the existing policy: matching
entries are refreshed in place, and new tags replace the non-MRU bank.

The lookahead path still reads both banks for the selected next fetch PC and
registers a full candidate PC, instruction word, valid bit, and bank bit. The
current IF cycle still selects live `io_imemData` whenever it is ready, uses a
replayed instruction only through the existing registered candidate and full
PC guard, and bubbles/holds on replay misses exactly as today. Reset still
clears bank valid bits, MRU bits, and candidate valid state; tag and
instruction payload arrays remain resetless behind valid bits.

This is intended as a pure timing micro-optimization. It should not change
CoreMark cycles, branch/JAL prediction policy, replay contents visible to the
pipeline, divider behavior, memory ordering, pending-store-slot semantics,
top-level IO, RVFI field widths, channel-0-only retirement, strict
`rvfi_order`, or channel-1 tie-off. The expected gain is a small Fmax
improvement from removing redundant live-fill tag compares and reducing
replay fill-bank selection depth in the current best IF block.

changes.description:
Add a one-bit registered replay candidate bank field. Use the normal
lookahead bank-hit result or the same-index fill-bypass bank to populate
it. Replace the current live-fill `bank0_match` / `bank1_match`
recomparisons against `pc` with a bank select based on the guarded
registered candidate hit; use the candidate bank for refresh hits and
the non-MRU bank for misses. Preserve the two 128-entry banks,
compressed high-PC tags, resetless payload arrays, full candidate-PC
guard, MRU replacement semantics, live-imem priority, fill bypass,
redirect priority, `fetch_ready` behavior, and static backward-branch
plus direct-JAL prediction.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to document that the registered
lookahead replay candidate also records the bank that produced it, and
that live fills reuse this candidate bank instead of recomparing the
current PC against both bank tags. Do not change bundle widths,
architectural state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, top-level
IO, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## What changed

- Added a one-bit registered replay candidate bank field in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Removed the live-fill `pc` tag recomparisons for bank 0 and bank 1.
- Live imem fills now refresh `replay_cand_bank_q` when the registered candidate is valid and guarded by `replay_cand_pc_q == pc`; otherwise they replace the non-MRU bank for the current index.
- Normal lookahead generation records the selected lookup-hit bank, and same-index fill bypass records the live fill bank.
- Updated the IF replay comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` and `rtl/if_stage.sv`; no bundle widths, top-level IO, RVFI fields, decoder behavior, or architectural state changed.

## Deviations

- No behavioral deviation from the hypothesis was intentional.
- The requested in-place `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not stage files in this sandbox because writes/removes under the original `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher` path were denied. I ran the same command in a dereferenced temporary copy under `/tmp` so the formal framework staging area was writable without modifying the protected source tree.

## Concerns

title: Pending Store Slot Load Forwarding
motivation:
The current best core already has the structural pieces that have survived
FPGA evaluation: a one-deep pending-store slot hides normal-RAM store
backpressure, and the banked registered instruction replay predictor hides
most random iStall bubbles. The structural experiments since then show the
boundary clearly. Larger store buffers and same-cycle refill failed
placement, same-word store coalescing did not reduce CoreMark cycles, and
data replay tables recovered some load-stall cycles but cost too much Fmax.

The remaining store-slot bottleneck is narrower. When a normal-RAM store has
retired into the pending slot, the core currently blocks every younger memory
operation until the physical write drains, even if the younger operation is a
load of bytes that are already fully present in the pending store entry. That
case does not require a second store-buffer entry or a dmem access: in
program order the store is already older and retired, so a same-word covered
load should read the store slot's bytes. CoreMark's stack, list, and state
code can produce store/load reuse on nearby words, especially under random
dmem stalls that keep the slot valid for multiple cycles.
hypothesis:
Add store-to-load forwarding from the existing pending normal-RAM store slot.
When the slot is valid and the MEM-stage instruction is a valid, legal,
aligned normal-RAM load whose word-aligned address matches the pending slot
word, allow the load to retire from the slot if its byte read mask is a
subset of the slot's pending write mask. The load must not drive the external
dmem port in that case; it uses the slot's replicated write data as the raw
word for sign/zero extension and RVFI `mem_rdata`, clears `mem_wait`, and
reports the load's own `mem_addr` and `mem_rmask` through MEM/WB.

Preserve all existing ordering constraints. Loads whose requested bytes are
not fully covered by the pending store, loads to a different word, younger
stores, MMIO/marker/UART accesses, misaligned or trapping memory operations,
and all non-normal-RAM accesses still wait behind a valid pending slot exactly
as today. The pending slot keeps priority on the physical dmem port while it
drains, including on the same cycle a covered load forwards from it. The
later physical drain of the store creates no extra RVFI event, and the
forwarded load retires once in normal channel-0 order.

This is a structural stall-hiding change, not a MEM-to-EX load-data bypass,
not a second store-buffer entry, and not a replay table. It keeps cached data
consumption at the MEM retirement boundary, so dependent younger
instructions still use the existing load-use discipline. The top-level IO,
RVFI NRET=2 field set, tied-off channel 1, strict `rvfi_order` sequence,
banked IF replay predictor, hot/cold ALU encoding, divider interlock, branch
recovery, decoder legality defaults, and timing-marker behavior remain
unchanged. The expected benefit is a small CoreMark cycle reduction under the

title: Skewed-Bank I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor is the two-bank registered lookahead instruction
replay table. It is the only predictor-family structure that has delivered a
large CoreMark gain and a strong Fmax result: it hides random imem stalls by
replaying hot instructions, while the current IF cycle still chooses only
between live imem data and an already registered candidate.

The remaining replay misses are likely conflict misses. The current two-bank
design stores two tags per raw `pc[8:2]` set, so any hot instructions spaced
by 512-byte multiples still contend for the same two physical slots. Earlier
alternatives show the tradeoff boundary: a single-bank XOR index changed the
conflict pattern but gave up the benefit of two-way capacity, and a victim
buffer added associative compares that cost more Fmax than the extra hits
recovered. A skewed second bank targets the same conflict class with no extra
table entries, no victim search, and no current-cycle table read.
hypothesis:
Keep the current two-bank registered lookahead replay predictor, but use
different indexes for the two banks. Bank 0 remains indexed by the raw
`pc[8:2]`. Bank 1 uses a small XOR-folded index such as
`pc[8:2] ^ pc[15:9]`. Both banks still store compressed high-PC tags
`pc[31:9]`, valid bits, and 32-bit instruction words behind resetless
payload arrays.

On live `io_imemReady=1` fetches, preserve live-imem-first behavior. If bank
0 at the raw index or bank 1 at the skewed index already matches the fetched
PC tag, refresh that matching bank. If neither bank matches, fill the
pseudo-LRU/non-MRU choice for that PC's raw set, using the raw-index MRU bit
as a replacement hint while writing bank 1 at its skewed index. This retains
the existing low-cost replacement state but lets the second candidate slot
come from a different conflict class.

During registered lookahead generation, read bank 0 at the raw index and
bank 1 at the skewed index for the selected next fetch PC, compare their
compressed tags, and register a replay candidate on either hit. The current
IF cycle remains unchanged: `fetch_ready` is driven only by live imem
readiness or the previously registered candidate guarded by the full
candidate PC match. Same-cycle fill bypass must use the same bank-specific
indexes so a just-fetched word can seed the next candidate when appropriate.

Correctness is still protected by the compressed tag plus the existing full
candidate-PC guard; a skew collision can only evict a useful replay word and
cause a miss. Static backward-branch and direct-JAL prediction, redirect
recovery, pending-store behavior, divider interlock, hot/cold ALU encoding,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, strict channel-0 order, and tied-off
channel 1 remain unchanged. The expected benefit is a small iStall cycle
reduction with limited Fmax risk because the extra XOR is only in the
registered lookahead/fill path, not the current-cycle replay-hit path.

changes.description:
Change the second replay bank to use a skewed index such as
`pc[8:2] ^ pc[15:9]`, while keeping bank 0 on the raw `pc[8:2]` index.
Apply the bank-specific indexes consistently to live fills, matching,
registered lookahead reads, and same-cycle fill bypass. Preserve the
two-bank table size, compressed high-PC tags, resetless payload arrays,
MRU replacement hint, registered candidate PC guard, live-imem priority,
replay-hit `fetch_ready` semantics, redirect priority, and the existing
static backward-branch plus direct-JAL prediction behavior.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to document that the registered
lookahead replay predictor uses one raw-index bank and one skew-index
bank, both protected by compressed high-PC tags and the registered
current-PC guard. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state,
decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the NRET=2
contract.

title: Resetless Replay MRU State
motivation:
The current best design is dominated by the two-bank registered instruction
replay predictor. It is the only predictor-family structure that has both
reduced random-iStall cycles and improved Fmax, and the successful
compressed-tag follow-up showed that narrow timing cleanups inside this IF
replay block can still move median fitness without changing CoreMark cycles.

The replay table already leaves tag and instruction payload arrays resetless
behind valid bits, but the per-index MRU replacement bits are still
synchronously reset. Those MRU bits are not architectural state and are not
needed for correctness after reset: both replay-bank valid bits are cleared,
so the first live fill for a set may choose either bank. After that fill, the
MRU bit is immediately written to the bank that was filled and normal
replacement behavior resumes.

Removing reset from this 128-bit replacement-state array is a narrower
version of the reset-elision idea that has already worked for replay
payloads. It targets reset fanout and local IF replay placement pressure
without changing the current-cycle fetch-ready path, replay hit rate after
warmup, branch/JAL prediction policy, ALU datapath, memory behavior, or RVFI
retirement.
hypothesis:
Make the two-bank instruction replay predictor's MRU replacement bits
resetless. On reset, continue clearing both replay-bank valid arrays and the
registered replay-candidate valid bit, but do not assign replay_mru entries.
Tag and instruction payload arrays remain resetless as today.

The fill-bank selection remains the same logical policy: refresh bank 0 or
bank 1 when the indexed high tag already matches, otherwise fill the
non-MRU bank and mark the filled bank MRU. With both valid bits cleared by
reset, an arbitrary resetless MRU value can only choose which empty bank gets
the first fill for a set. It cannot make a replay hit valid for the wrong PC,
because replay selection still requires a bank valid bit, compressed tag
match, registered candidate valid bit, and full candidate-PC guard.

Live io_imemData still has priority whenever io_imemReady is high, replay
misses still hold the PC and inject the existing IF bubble, and replay hits
still feed the selected instruction through the existing static
backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction path. The top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2
port set, channel-0-only retirement convention, strict rvfi_order sequence,
pending-store slot, divider interlock, hot/cold ALU encoding, decoder
legality rules, and channel-1 tie-off remain unchanged.

CoreMark cycle count should be unchanged. The expected benefit is a small
median Fmax improvement from removing reset muxing and reset routing on the
replay MRU bits in the timing-sensitive IF replay block.

changes.description:
Remove replay_mru from the reset loop so only replay_valid_b0,
replay_valid_b1, and replay_cand_valid_q are reset. Keep replay_mru
updated on every live imem fill exactly as today, and preserve the
two-bank table structure, compressed high-PC tags, resetless tag/data
payload arrays, registered candidate PC guard, live-imem priority,
same-index fill bypass, redirect priority, fetch_ready semantics, and
static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction behavior.

Update only IF/ID replay comments to document that replay bank tags,
instruction payloads, and MRU replacement bits are resetless behind
reset-cleared valid bits. Do not change bundle widths, architectural
state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, top-level IO, or the
NRET=2 contract.
